**Milan**[[a]](#cite_note-8) ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Milano*)[[b]](#cite_note-9) is the regional capital of [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy"), in [northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy"), and the seat of the [Metropolitan City of Milan](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Milan "Metropolitan City of Milan"). It is the [second-most populous city in Italy](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Italy "List of cities in Italy") after [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), with a population of 1.36 million in 2025. The city's [wider metropolitan area](/wiki/Milan_metropolitan_area "Milan metropolitan area") is the largest in Italy, and the [fourth-largest in the European Union](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_in_Europe "List of metropolitan areas in Europe"), with an estimated population of 6.1 million.[[3]](#cite_note-CityPopulation.de-3) Milan is considered Italy's economic capital,[[8]](#cite_note-10) and its metropolitan area accounts for about 20% of [the country's GDP](/wiki/Economy_of_Italy "Economy of Italy").[[9]](#cite_note-prologis-11)

Founded around 590 BC[[10]](#cite_note-cronologia-12) by a [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") tribe, Milan [was conquered](/wiki/Roman_expansion_in_Italy "Roman expansion in Italy") by the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") in 222 BC, who [Latinized](/wiki/Latinisation_of_names "Latinisation of names") the name of the city into [Mediolanum](/wiki/Mediolanum "Mediolanum").[[10]](#cite_note-cronologia-12)[[11]](#cite_note-Tellier-13) At the end of the fourth century, it briefly served as capital of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire").[[12]](#cite_note-14) In the Late Medieval period, the wealthy [Duchy of Milan](/wiki/Duchy_of_Milan "Duchy of Milan") was one of the greatest forces behind the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance").[[13]](#cite_note-:0-15)[[14]](#cite_note-16) As a major center of the [Italian Enlightenment](/wiki/Italian_Enlightenment "Italian Enlightenment") during the [Early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"), Milan's cultural and political struggle against [Austrian](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") domination was crucial in the reunification of the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"). From the 19th century onwards, Milan led the industrial and financial development of Italy.[[15]](#cite_note-17)[[16]](#cite_note-18)

Milan is a major international center of industry, finance,[[17]](#cite_note-19) science,[[18]](#cite_note-20) communications, fashion, art and tourism. Milan was classified as an "Alpha" city by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network").[[19]](#cite_note-21) Milan's business district hosts [Borsa Italiana](/wiki/Borsa_Italiana "Borsa Italiana"), Italy's main stock exchange (part of the [Euronext](/wiki/Euronext "Euronext") consortium, the world's [sixth-largest](/wiki/List_of_major_stock_exchanges "List of major stock exchanges") by market capitalization) and the headquarters of numerous national corporations, including eight [*Fortune* 500 Europe](/wiki/List_of_largest_Italian_companies "List of largest Italian companies") companies.[[20]](#cite_note-22) As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit), Milan and its [special metropolitan authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Milan "Metropolitan City of Milan") have the largest GDP and the highest per-capita GDP of any Italian province.[[21]](#cite_note-23)

Milan is a global fashion capital[[22]](#cite_note-Breward-24) and a major international tourist destination, being one of the most visited cities in the world, ranked second in Italy after Rome, fifth in Europe and sixteenth in the world.[[23]](#cite_note-ilgiorno-25)[[24]](#cite_note-newsroom-26) The city is a major cultural center, with museums and art galleries that feature some of the most important collections in the world, including major works by [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci").[[25]](#cite_note-27)[[26]](#cite_note-28) Milan hosts numerous educational institutions, including academies and universities that account for 11% of the national total of enrolled students.[[27]](#cite_note-University_and_research_in_Milan-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30) Milan hosts several international events and fairs, including [Milan Fashion Week](/wiki/Milan_Fashion_Week "Milan Fashion Week") and the [Milan Furniture Fair](/wiki/Milan_Furniture_Fair "Milan Furniture Fair"), which are among the world's largest in terms of revenue, visitors and growth.[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33) Milan has many luxury hotels and is the fifth most starred in the world by [Michelin Guide](/wiki/Michelin_Guide "Michelin Guide").[[32]](#cite_note-34) It hosted the [Universal Exposition](/wiki/World%27s_fair "World's fair") in [1906](/wiki/Milan_International "Milan International") and [2015](/wiki/Expo_2015 "Expo 2015"). In the field of sports, Milan is home to two of Europe's most successful football teams, [AC Milan](/wiki/AC_Milan "AC Milan") and [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan"), and one of Europe's main basketball teams, [Olimpia Milano](/wiki/Olimpia_Milano "Olimpia Milano"). The city also co-hosted the [Winter Olympic](/wiki/2026_Winter_Olympics "2026 Winter Olympics") and [Paralympic](/wiki/2026_Winter_Paralympics "2026 Winter Paralympics") games in 2026.[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-37)

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Scrofa_semilanuta.jpg/250px-Scrofa_semilanuta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scrofa_semilanuta.jpg)

A bas-relief on the [Palazzo della Ragione](/wiki/Palazzo_della_Ragione,_Milan "Palazzo della Ragione, Milan") of the *[scrofa semilanuta](/wiki/Scrofa_semilanuta "Scrofa semilanuta")* ("half-woolly sow") from which, according to a theory, the city's toponym may derive

Milan was founded with the Celtic name of Medhelanon,[[11]](#cite_note-Tellier-13)[[10]](#cite_note-cronologia-12) later [Latinized](/wiki/Latinisation_of_names "Latinisation of names") by the [ancient Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Romans "Ancient Romans") into [Mediolanum](/wiki/Mediolanum "Mediolanum"). In [Celtic language](/wiki/Celtic_language "Celtic language") *medhe-* meant "middle, center" and the name element *-lanon* is the Celtic equivalent of Latin *-planum* "plain", meant "(settlement) in the midst of the plain",[[36]](#cite_note-Delamarre_2003_https://archive.org/details/dictionnairedela00dela_348/page/n219_221–222-38)[[37]](#cite_note-Quintela-39) or of "place between watercourses" (Celtic *medhe* = "in the middle, central"; *land* or *lan* = "land"), given the presence of the [Olona](/wiki/Olona "Olona"), [Lambro](/wiki/Lambro "Lambro"), [Seveso](/wiki/Seveso_(river) "Seveso (river)") rivers and the Nirone and Pudiga streams.[[38]](#cite_note-CraccoRuggini17-40)

The [Latin](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") name *Mediolanum* comes from the Latin words *medio* (in the middle) and *planus* (plain).[[39]](#cite_note-41) Some scholars believe that *lanum* comes from the [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") root *lan*, meaning an enclosure or demarcated territory (source of the [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") word *[llan](/wiki/Llan_(placename) "Llan (placename)")*, meaning "a sanctuary or church", ultimately cognate to English/German *Land*) in which Celtic communities used to build shrines.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

Hence *Mediolanum* could signify the central town or sanctuary of a Celtic tribe. About sixty Gallo-Roman sites in France bore the name "Mediolanum", for example: [Saintes](/wiki/Saintes,_Charente-Maritime "Saintes, Charente-Maritime") (*[Mediolanum Santonum](/wiki/Mediolanum_Santonum "Mediolanum Santonum")*) and [Évreux](/wiki/%C3%89vreux "Évreux") (*[Mediolanum Aulercorum](/wiki/Mediolanum_Aulercorum "Mediolanum Aulercorum")*).[[41]](#cite_note-43) Another theory links the name to the *[scrofa semilanuta](/wiki/Scrofa_semilanuta "Scrofa semilanuta")* ("half-woolly sow") an ancient emblem of the city, fancifully accounted for in [Andrea Alciato](/wiki/Andrea_Alciato "Andrea Alciato")'s *Emblemata* (1584), beneath a woodcut of the first raising of the city walls, where a boar is seen lifted from the excavation, and the etymology of *Mediolanum* given as "half-wool",[[42]](#cite_note-44) explained in Latin and in French.

According to this theory, the foundation of Milan is credited to two [Celtic peoples](/wiki/Celts "Celts"), the [Bituriges](/wiki/Bituriges_Cubi "Bituriges Cubi") and the [Aedui](/wiki/Aedui "Aedui"), having as their [emblems](/wiki/Emblem "Emblem") a ram and a boar.[[43]](#cite_note-45) Therefore "The [city's symbol](/wiki/Symbols_of_Milan "Symbols of Milan") is a wool-bearing boar, an animal of double form, here with sharp bristles, there with sleek wool."[[44]](#cite_note-46) Alciato credits [Ambrose](/wiki/Ambrose "Ambrose") for his account.[[45]](#cite_note-47)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Milan](/wiki/History_of_Milan "History of Milan")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Milan](/wiki/Timeline_of_Milan "Timeline of Milan").

### Celtic era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Celtic era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/9754_-_Museo_archeologico_di_Milano_-_Olletta_celtica_%28sec._II-I_a.C.%29_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto%2C_13-mar-2012.jpg/250px-9754_-_Museo_archeologico_di_Milano_-_Olletta_celtica_%28sec._II-I_a.C.%29_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto%2C_13-mar-2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:9754_-_Museo_archeologico_di_Milano_-_Olletta_celtica_(sec._II-I_a.C.)_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto,_13-mar-2012.jpg)

[Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") artifacts from the period before the Roman conquest (3rd–2nd centuries BC), preserved in the [Civic Archaeological Museum of Milan](/wiki/Archaeological_Museum,_Milan "Archaeological Museum, Milan")

Around 590 BC[[10]](#cite_note-cronologia-12) a [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") tribe belonging to the [Insubres](/wiki/Insubre "Insubre") group and belonging to the [Golasecca culture](/wiki/Golasecca_culture "Golasecca culture") settled the city under the name Medhelanon.[[11]](#cite_note-Tellier-13)[[10]](#cite_note-cronologia-12) According to the legend reported by [Livy](/wiki/Livy "Livy") (writing between 27 and 9 BC), the [Gaulish](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul") king [Ambicatus](/wiki/Ambicatus "Ambicatus") sent his nephew [Bellovesus](/wiki/Bellovesus "Bellovesus") into northern Italy at the head of a party drawn from various Gaulish tribes; Bellovesus allegedly founded the settlement in the times of the Roman monarchy, during the reign of [Tarquinius Priscus](/wiki/Lucius_Tarquinius_Priscus "Lucius Tarquinius Priscus"). Tarquin is traditionally recorded as reigning from 616 to 579 BC, according to ancient Roman historian Titus Livy.[[46]](#cite_note-48)

Medhelanon, in particular, was developed around a [sanctuary](/wiki/Sanctuary "Sanctuary"), which was the oldest area of the village.[[47]](#cite_note-Tosi-49) The sanctuary, which consisted of a wooded area in the shape of an ellipse with a central clearing, was aligned according to precise astronomical points. For this reason, it was used for religious gatherings, especially in particular celebratory moments. The sanctuary of Medhelanon was an ellipse with axes of 443 m (1,453 ft) and 323 m (1,060 ft) located near [Piazza della Scala](/wiki/Piazza_della_Scala "Piazza della Scala").[[47]](#cite_note-Tosi-49)

The urban planning profile was based on these early paths, and on the shape of the sanctuary, reached, in some cases, up to the 19th century and even beyond. For example, the route of the modern Corso Vittorio Emanuele, [Piazza del Duomo](/wiki/Piazza_del_Duomo,_Milan "Piazza del Duomo, Milan"), [Piazza Cordusio](/wiki/Piazza_Cordusio "Piazza Cordusio") and Via Broletto, which is curvilinear, could correspond to the south side of the ellipse of the ancient sanctuary of Medhelanon.[[47]](#cite_note-Tosi-49)

One axis of the Medhelanon sanctuary was aligned towards the [heliacal rising](/wiki/Heliacal_rising "Heliacal rising") of [Antares](/wiki/Antares "Antares"), while the other towards the heliacal rising of [Capella](/wiki/Capella "Capella"). The latter coincided with a Celtic spring festival celebrated on 24 March, while the heliacal rising of Antares corresponded with 11 November, which opened and closed the Celtic year and which coincided with the point where the Sun rose on the winter [solstice](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice").[[47]](#cite_note-Tosi-49) About two centuries after the creation of the Celtic sanctuary, the first residential settlements began to be built around it. Medhelanon then transformed from a simple religious center to an urban and then military centre, thus becoming a real village.[[47]](#cite_note-Tosi-49)

The first homes were built just south of the Celtic sanctuary, near the modern [Royal Palace of Milan](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Milan "Royal Palace of Milan").[[47]](#cite_note-Tosi-49) Subsequently, with the growth of the town centre, other important buildings for the Medhelanon community were built. First, a temple dedicated to the goddess [Belisama](/wiki/Belisama "Belisama") was built, which was located near the modern [Milan Cathedral](/wiki/Milan_Cathedral "Milan Cathedral"). Then, near the modern Via Moneta, which is located near today's [Piazza San Sepolcro](/wiki/Piazza_San_Sepolcro "Piazza San Sepolcro"), a fortified building with military functions was built which was surrounded by a defensive moat.[[47]](#cite_note-Tosi-49)

### Roman times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Roman times")]

Main article: [Mediolanum](/wiki/Mediolanum "Mediolanum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Colonne_di_san_lorenzo_01.jpg/250px-Colonne_di_san_lorenzo_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colonne_di_san_lorenzo_01.jpg)

Roman ruins in Milan: the [Columns of San Lorenzo](/wiki/Colonne_di_San_Lorenzo "Colonne di San Lorenzo")

During the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic"), the Romans, led by consul [Gnaeus Cornelius Scipio Calvus](/wiki/Gnaeus_Cornelius_Scipio_Calvus "Gnaeus Cornelius Scipio Calvus"), fought the [Insubres](/wiki/Insubres "Insubres") and captured the settlement in 222 BC. The chief of the Insubres then submitted to Rome, giving the Romans control of the settlement.[[48]](#cite_note-50) The Romans eventually [conquered the entirety of the region](/wiki/Roman_expansion_in_Italy "Roman expansion in Italy"), calling the new [province](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province") "[Cisalpine Gaul](/wiki/Cisalpine_Gaul "Cisalpine Gaul")" ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Gallia Cisalpina*)—"Gaul this side of the Alps"—and may have given the city its Latinized name of Mediolanum: in [Gaulish](/wiki/Gaulish "Gaulish") *\*medio-* meant "middle, centre" and the name element *-lanon* is the Celtic equivalent of Latin *-planum* "plain", thus *\*Mediolanon* (Latinized as *Mediolānum*) meant "(settlement) in the midst of the plain".[[37]](#cite_note-Quintela-39)[[36]](#cite_note-Delamarre_2003_https://archive.org/details/dictionnairedela00dela_348/page/n219_221–222-38) Mediolanum became the most important center of Cisalpine Gaul and, in the wake of economic development, in 49 BC, was elevated, within the *[Lex Roscia](/wiki/Lex_Roscia "Lex Roscia")*, to the status of *[municipium](/wiki/Municipium "Municipium")*.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/dd/Ruins-imperial-complex-milan-.jpg/250px-Ruins-imperial-complex-milan-.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ruins-imperial-complex-milan-.jpg)

Ruins of the Emperor's palace in Milan located in Via Gorani. Here [Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") and [Licinius](/wiki/Licinius "Licinius") issued the [Edict of Milan](/wiki/Edict_of_Milan "Edict of Milan").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Civico_museo_archeologico_di_Milano_1.jpg/250px-Civico_museo_archeologico_di_Milano_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Civico_museo_archeologico_di_Milano_1.jpg)

Remains of the [Roman walls of Milan](/wiki/Walls_of_Milan#Roman_walls "Walls of Milan") located inside the [Civic Archaeological Museum of Milan](/wiki/Archaeological_Museum,_Milan "Archaeological Museum, Milan")

The ancient Celtic settlement was, from a [topographic](/wiki/Topography "Topography") point of view, superimposed and replaced by the Roman one. The Roman city was then gradually superimposed and replaced by the medieval one. The urban center of Milan has therefore grown constantly and rapidly, until modern times, around the first Celtic nucleus. The original Celtic toponym Medhelanon then changed, as evidenced by a graffiti in Celtic language present on a section of the Roman walls of Milan which dates back to a period following the Roman conquest of the Celtic village, in Mesiolano.[[50]](#cite_note-romanoimpero-52) In 286, the Roman Emperor [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian") moved the capital of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") from Rome to Mediolanum.[[51]](#cite_note-53) Diocletian himself chose to reside at [Nicomedia](/wiki/Nicomedia "Nicomedia") in the Eastern Empire, leaving his colleague [Maximian](/wiki/Maximian "Maximian") at Milan.

During the Augustan age Mediolanum was famous for its schools; it possessed a theatre and an [amphitheatre](/wiki/Milan_amphitheatre "Milan amphitheatre") (129.5 x 109.3 m), the third largest in [Roman Italy](/wiki/Roman_Italy "Roman Italy") after the [Colosseum](/wiki/Colosseum "Colosseum") in Rome and the vast amphitheatre in [Capua](/wiki/Capua "Capua").[[52]](#cite_note-54) A large stone wall encircled the city in Caesar's time, and later was expanded in the late third century AD, by Maximian. Maximian built several gigantic monuments including the large [circus](/wiki/Roman_circus "Roman circus") (470 × 85 metres) and the *[thermae](/wiki/Thermae "Thermae")* or [Baths of Hercules](/wiki/Baths_of_Hercules "Baths of Hercules"), a large complex of imperial palaces and other services and buildings of which few visible traces remain. Maximian increased the city area to 375 acres by surrounding it with a new, larger stone wall (about 4.5 km long) with many 24-sided towers. The monumental area had twin towers; the one included later in the construction of the convent of [San Maurizio Maggiore](/wiki/San_Maurizio_al_Monastero_Maggiore "San Maurizio al Monastero Maggiore") remains 16.6 m high.

It was from Mediolanum that the [Emperor Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") issued what is now known as the [Edict of Milan](/wiki/Edict_of_Milan "Edict of Milan") in AD 313, granting tolerance to all religions within the Empire, thus paving the way for Christianity to become the dominant religion of the Empire. Constantine was in Mediolanum to celebrate the wedding of his sister to the Eastern Emperor, [Licinius](/wiki/Licinius "Licinius"). In 402, the [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") besieged the city and the [Emperor Honorius](/wiki/Honorius_(emperor) "Honorius (emperor)") moved the Imperial residence to [Ravenna](/wiki/Ravenna "Ravenna").[[53]](#cite_note-55) In 452, [Attila](/wiki/Attila "Attila") besieged the city, but the real break with the city's Imperial past came in 539, during the [Gothic War](/wiki/Gothic_War_(535%E2%80%93554) "Gothic War (535–554)"), when [Uraias](/wiki/Uraias "Uraias") (a nephew of [Witiges](/wiki/Vitiges "Vitiges"), formerly King of the [Italian Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogothic_Kingdom "Ostrogothic Kingdom")) carried out attacks in Milan, with losses, according to [Procopius](/wiki/Procopius "Procopius"), being about 300,000 men. The [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") took [Ticinum](/wiki/Ticinum "Ticinum") as their capital in 572 (renaming it *Papia* – the modern [Pavia](/wiki/Pavia "Pavia")), and left [early-medieval](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages") Milan to the governance of its [archbishops](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Milan "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Milan").

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

Main article: [Lordship of Milan](/wiki/Lordship_of_Milan "Lordship of Milan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/5903_-_Milano_-_Camillo_Boito%2C_Porta_Ticinese_%281865%29_-Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto_27-Feb-2007.jpg/250px-5903_-_Milano_-_Camillo_Boito%2C_Porta_Ticinese_%281865%29_-Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto_27-Feb-2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:5903_-_Milano_-_Camillo_Boito,_Porta_Ticinese_(1865)_-Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto_27-Feb-2007.jpg)

The 12th century [Medieval Porta Ticinese](/wiki/Porta_Ticinese_(Medieval_Gate_of_Milan) "Porta Ticinese (Medieval Gate of Milan)") is one of the city's three medieval gates that still exist in modern Milan.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Piazza_mercanti_Milano.JPG/250px-Piazza_mercanti_Milano.JPG)](/wiki/File:Piazza_mercanti_Milano.JPG)

[Piazza Mercanti](/wiki/Piazza_Mercanti "Piazza Mercanti") was the heart of the city in the Middle Ages.

After the siege of the city by the Visigoths in 402, the imperial residence moved to Ravenna. Attila, King of the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns"), [sacked and devastated the city](/wiki/Siege_of_Milan_(452) "Siege of Milan (452)") in 452 AD. In 539 the [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths") conquered and destroyed Milan during the Gothic War against [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") Emperor [Justinian I](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I"). In the summer of 569 the Lombards (from whom the name of the Italian region [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy") derives), conquered Milan, overpowering the small [Byzantine garrison](/wiki/Byzantine_army "Byzantine army") left for its defence. Some Roman structures remained in use in Milan under Lombard rule.[[54]](#cite_note-56) Milan surrendered to [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") and the [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") in 774.

The 11th century saw a reaction against the control of the [Holy Roman Emperors](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor"). City-states emerged in northern Italy, an expression of the new political power of the cities and their will to fight against all feudal powers. Milan was no exception. It did not take long, however, for the Italian city-states to begin fighting each other to try to limit neighbouring powers.[[55]](#cite_note-57) The Milanese destroyed Lodi and continuously warred with Pavia, Cremona and Como, who in turn asked [Frederick I Barbarossa](/wiki/Frederick_I_Barbarossa "Frederick I Barbarossa") for help. In a sally they captured [Empress Beatrice](/wiki/Beatrice_I,_Countess_of_Burgundy "Beatrice I, Countess of Burgundy") and forced her to [ride a donkey](/wiki/Parading_on_donkey "Parading on donkey") backward through the city until getting out. Frederick I Barbarossa brought the destruction of much of Milan in 1162.[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59)

A period of peace followed and Milan prospered as a centre of trade due to its geographical position. During this time, the city was considered one of the largest European cities.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-15) As a result of the independence that the Lombard cities gained in the [Peace of Constance](/wiki/Peace_of_Constance "Peace of Constance") in 1183, Milan returned to the commune form of local government first established in the 11th century.[[58]](#cite_note-60)[[59]](#cite_note-61)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Ducato_di_Milano%2C_1402.svg/250px-Ducato_di_Milano%2C_1402.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ducato_di_Milano,_1402.svg)

The [Duchy of Milan](/wiki/Duchy_of_Milan "Duchy of Milan") (1395–1447 and 1450–1796) at the time of [Gian Galeazzo Visconti](/wiki/Gian_Galeazzo_Visconti "Gian Galeazzo Visconti")'s death in 1402. The [Golden Ambrosian Republic](/wiki/Golden_Ambrosian_Republic "Golden Ambrosian Republic") (1447–1450) briefly existed between the end of the [Visconti](/wiki/Visconti_of_Milan "Visconti of Milan") period in 1447 and the start of the [Sforza](/wiki/House_of_Sforza "House of Sforza") period in 1450.

In 1395, [Gian Galeazzo Visconti](/wiki/Gian_Galeazzo_Visconti "Gian Galeazzo Visconti") became the first [Duke of Milan](/wiki/List_of_rulers_of_Milan "List of rulers of Milan") upon receiving the title from [Wenceslaus, King of the Romans](/wiki/Wenceslaus,_King_of_the_Romans "Wenceslaus, King of the Romans"). In 1447, [Filippo Maria Visconti](/wiki/Filippo_Maria_Visconti "Filippo Maria Visconti"), Duke of Milan, died without a male heir. Following the end of the Visconti line, the [Ambrosian Republic](/wiki/Golden_Ambrosian_Republic "Golden Ambrosian Republic") was established; it took its name from St. Ambrose, the popular patron saint of the city.[[60]](#cite_note-lucas:268-62) Both the Guelph and the Ghibelline factions worked together to bring about the Ambrosian Republic in Milan. In 1450, the Republic collapsed when Milan was conquered by [Francesco I](/wiki/Francesco_I_Sforza "Francesco I Sforza") of the [House of Sforza](/wiki/House_of_Sforza "House of Sforza"), which made Milan one of the leading cities of the Italian [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance").[[60]](#cite_note-lucas:268-62)[[61]](#cite_note-InternationalStudent-63) Under the House of Sforza, Milan experienced a period of great prosperity, which in particular saw the development of mulberry cultivation and silk processing.[[62]](#cite_note-64)

Following this economic growth, works such as the [Sforza Castle](/wiki/Sforza_Castle "Sforza Castle") (already existing in the Visconti era under the name of Porta Giovia Castle, but re-adapted, enlarged and completed by the Sforza family) and the [Ospedale Maggiore](/wiki/Policlinico_of_Milan "Policlinico of Milan") were completed. The Sforzas also managed to attract to Milan personalities such as [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"), who redesigned and improved the function of the [navigli](/wiki/Navigli "Navigli") and painted *[The Last Supper](/wiki/The_Last_Supper_(Leonardo) "The Last Supper (Leonardo)")*, and [Bramante](/wiki/Bramante "Bramante"), who worked on the [church of Santa Maria presso San Satiro](/wiki/Santa_Maria_presso_San_Satiro "Santa Maria presso San Satiro"), on the [basilica of Sant'Ambrogio](/wiki/Basilica_of_Sant%27Ambrogio "Basilica of Sant'Ambrogio") and to the [church of Santa Maria delle Grazie](/wiki/Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie,_Milan "Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan"), influencing the development of the [Lombard Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_in_Lombardy "Renaissance in Lombardy").

### Early modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early modern")]

Main article: [Duchy of Milan](/wiki/Duchy_of_Milan "Duchy of Milan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Milan..._-_btv1b8459040r.jpg/250px-Milan..._-_btv1b8459040r.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milan..._-_btv1b8459040r.jpg)

A 1680 map of Milan, with the [Spanish walls](/wiki/Walls_of_Milan#Spanish_walls "Walls of Milan") encircling the city

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/1807KingdomItaly.jpg/250px-1807KingdomItaly.jpg)](/wiki/File:1807KingdomItaly.jpg)

Milan was the capital of the [Napoleonic Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(Napoleonic) "Kingdom of Italy (Napoleonic)"), highlighted in yellow

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/9036_-_Milano%2C_C.so_Venezia_-_Giuseppe_Sommaruga%2C_Pal._Castiglioni_%281904%29_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto_22-Apr-2007.jpg/250px-9036_-_Milano%2C_C.so_Venezia_-_Giuseppe_Sommaruga%2C_Pal._Castiglioni_%281904%29_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto_22-Apr-2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:9036_-_Milano,_C.so_Venezia_-_Giuseppe_Sommaruga,_Pal._Castiglioni_(1904)_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto_22-Apr-2007.jpg)

Historic *[palazzi](/wiki/Palazzo "Palazzo")* on [Corso Venezia](/wiki/Corso_Venezia "Corso Venezia")

Milan's last independent ruler, [Lodovico il Moro](/wiki/Lodovico_il_Moro "Lodovico il Moro"), requested the aid of [Charles VIII of France](/wiki/Charles_VIII_of_France "Charles VIII of France") against the other [Italian states](/wiki/Italian_states "Italian states"), eventually unleashing the [Italian Wars](/wiki/Italian_Wars "Italian Wars"). The king's cousin, [Louis of Orléans](/wiki/Louis_XII "Louis XII"), took part in the expedition and realized most of Italy was virtually defenseless. This prompted him to come back a few years later in 1500, and claim the Duchy of Milan for himself, his grandmother having been a member of the ruling [Visconti](/wiki/Visconti_of_Milan "Visconti of Milan") family. At that time, Milan was also defended by [Swiss mercenaries](/wiki/Swiss_mercenaries "Swiss mercenaries"). After the victory of Louis's successor François I over the Swiss at the [Battle of Marignan](/wiki/Battle_of_Marignan "Battle of Marignan"), the duchy was promised to the French king [François I](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_I_of_France "François I of France"). When the Spanish Habsburg Emperor [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") defeated François I at the [Battle of Pavia](/wiki/Battle_of_Pavia "Battle of Pavia") in 1525, [northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy"), which included Milan, passed to [Habsburg Spain](/wiki/Hapsburg_Spain "Hapsburg Spain").[[63]](#cite_note-65)

In 1556, Charles V abdicated in favour of his son [Philip II](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") and his brother [Ferdinand I](/wiki/Ferdinand_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Ferdinand I, Holy Roman Emperor"). Charles's Italian possessions, including Milan, passed to Philip II and remained with the Spanish line of Habsburgs, while Ferdinand's Austrian line of Habsburgs ruled the Holy Roman Empire. The [Great Plague of Milan](/wiki/Italian_plague_of_1629%E2%80%931631 "Italian plague of 1629–1631") in 1629–31, that claimed the lives of an estimated 60,000 people out of a population of 130,000, caused unprecedented devastation in the city and was effectively described by [Alessandro Manzoni](/wiki/Alessandro_Manzoni "Alessandro Manzoni") in his masterpiece *[The Betrothed](/wiki/The_Betrothed "The Betrothed")*. This episode was seen by many as the symbol of Spanish bad rule and decadence and is considered one of the last outbreaks of the centuries-long [pandemic](/wiki/Pandemic "Pandemic") of plague that began with the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death").[[64]](#cite_note-66)

In 1700, the Spanish line of Habsburgs was extinguished with the death of [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Spain "Charles II of Spain"). After his death, the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") began in 1701. In 1706, the French were defeated in [Ramillies](/wiki/Battle_of_Ramillies "Battle of Ramillies") and [Turin](/wiki/Siege_of_Turin "Siege of Turin") and were forced to yield northern Italy to the [Austrian Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"). In 1713 and 1714, the Treaties of [Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht") and [Rastatt](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rastatt "Treaty of Rastatt") formally confirmed Austrian sovereignty over most of Habsburg Spain's Italian possessions including Lombardy and its capital, Milan.

[Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") invaded Italy in 1796, and Milan was declared capital of the [Cisalpine Republic](/wiki/Cisalpine_Republic "Cisalpine Republic"). Later, he declared Milan capital of the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(Napoleonic) "Kingdom of Italy (Napoleonic)") and was crowned King of Italy in the cathedral. After Napoleon's occupation ended, the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") returned Lombardy and Milan to Austrian control in 1815.[[65]](#cite_note-67)

### Late modern and contemporary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Late modern and contemporary")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Cinque_giornate_di_Milano.jpg/250px-Cinque_giornate_di_Milano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cinque_giornate_di_Milano.jpg)

A popular print depicting the "[Five Days of Milan](/wiki/Five_Days_of_Milan "Five Days of Milan")" (18–22 March 1848) uprising against Austrian rule

On 18 March 1848, Milan effectively rebelled against Austrian rule, during the so-called "[Five Days](/wiki/Five_Days_of_Milan "Five Days of Milan")" ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Le Cinque Giornate*), that forced Field Marshal [Radetzky](/wiki/Joseph_Radetzky_von_Radetz "Joseph Radetzky von Radetz") to temporarily withdraw from the city. The bordering [Kingdom of Piedmont–Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)") sent troops to protect the insurgents and organised a [plebiscite](/wiki/Plebiscite "Plebiscite") that ratified by a huge majority the unification of Lombardy with Piedmont–Sardinia. But just a few months later the Austrians were able to send fresh forces that routed the Piedmontese army at the [Battle of Custoza](/wiki/Battle_of_Custoza_(1848) "Battle of Custoza (1848)") on 24 July and to reassert Austrian control over northern Italy. About ten years later, however, Italian nationalist politicians, officers and intellectuals such as [Cavour](/wiki/Camillo_Benso,_Count_of_Cavour "Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour"), [Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi") and [Mazzini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Mazzini "Giuseppe Mazzini") were able to gather a huge consensus and to pressure the monarchy to forge an alliance with the new [French Empire](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire") of [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") to defeat Austria and establish a large Italian state in the region. At the [Battle of Solferino](/wiki/Battle_of_Solferino "Battle of Solferino") in 1859, French and Italian troops heavily defeated the Austrians that retreated under the [Quadrilateral line](/wiki/Quadrilatero "Quadrilatero").[[66]](#cite_note-Solferino-68) Following this battle, Milan and the rest of Lombardy were incorporated into Piedmont-Sardinia, which then proceeded to annex all the other Italian statelets and proclaim the birth of the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") on 17 March 1861.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Piazza_Cordusio_a_Milano%2C_veduta_generale.jpg/250px-Piazza_Cordusio_a_Milano%2C_veduta_generale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piazza_Cordusio_a_Milano,_veduta_generale.jpg)

[Piazza Cordusio](/wiki/Piazza_Cordusio "Piazza Cordusio") in Milan

The political [unification of Italy](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy") enhanced Milan's economic dominance over northern Italy. A dense rail network, whose construction had started under Austrian patronage, was completed in a brief time, making Milan the rail hub of northern Italy and, with the opening of the [Gotthard](/wiki/Gotthard_Rail_Tunnel "Gotthard Rail Tunnel") (1882) and [Simplon](/wiki/Simplon_Tunnel "Simplon Tunnel") (1906) railway tunnels, the major South European rail hub for goods and passenger transport. Indeed, Milan and Venice were among the main stops of the [Orient Express](/wiki/Orient_Express "Orient Express") that started operating from 1919.[[67]](#cite_note-69) Abundant hydroelectric resources allowed the development of a strong steel and textile sector and, as Milanese banks dominated Italy's financial sphere, the city became the country's leading financial centre. In May 1898, Milan was shaken by the [Bava Beccaris massacre](/wiki/Bava_Beccaris_massacre "Bava Beccaris massacre"), a riot related to soaring cost of living.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Milano%2C_Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II_%28bombardata%29_02.jpg/250px-Milano%2C_Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II_%28bombardata%29_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milano,_Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II_(bombardata)_02.jpg)

The [Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II](/wiki/Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II "Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II") destroyed by [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") bombings, 1943

Milan's northern location in Italy closer to Europe, secured also a leading role for the city on the political scene. It was in Milan that [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") built his political and journalistic careers, and his fascist [Blackshirts](/wiki/Blackshirts "Blackshirts") rallied for the first time in the city's Piazza San Sepolcro; here the future [Fascist](/wiki/Fascist "Fascist") dictator launched his [March on Rome](/wiki/March_on_Rome "March on Rome") on 28 October 1922. During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") Milan's large industrial and transport facilities [suffered extensive damage from Allied bombings](/wiki/Bombing_of_Milan_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Milan in World War II") that often also hit residential districts.[[69]](#cite_note-71) When Italy [surrendered](/wiki/Armistice_between_Italy_and_Allied_armed_forces "Armistice between Italy and Allied armed forces") in 1943, German forces occupied and plundered most of northern Italy, fueling the birth of a massive resistance guerrilla movement.[[70]](#cite_note-Cooke-72) On 29 April 1945, the American [1st Armored Division](/wiki/1st_Armored_Division_(United_States) "1st Armored Division (United States)") was advancing on Milan but, before it arrived, the Italian resistance seized control of the city and [executed Mussolini](/wiki/Death_of_Benito_Mussolini "Death of Benito Mussolini") along with his mistress and several regime officers, that were later hanged and exposed in [Piazzale Loreto](/wiki/Piazzale_Loreto "Piazzale Loreto"), where one year before some resistance members had been executed.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Corso_Vittorio_Emanuele_II_-_Milan%2C_Italy_-_December_6%2C_2009_01.jpg/250px-Corso_Vittorio_Emanuele_II_-_Milan%2C_Italy_-_December_6%2C_2009_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Corso_Vittorio_Emanuele_II_-_Milan,_Italy_-_December_6,_2009_01.jpg)

Corso Vittorio Emanuele II, one of the main shopping streets in Milan, with the [Duomo](/wiki/Milan_Cathedral "Milan Cathedral") at left and [La Rinascente](/wiki/La_Rinascente "La Rinascente") department store at right

During the post-war economic boom, the reconstruction effort and the [Italian economic miracle](/wiki/Italian_economic_miracle "Italian economic miracle") attracted a large wave of internal migration (especially from rural areas of [southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy")) to Milan. The population grew from 1.3 million in 1951 to 1.7 million in 1967.[[71]](#cite_note-Ginsborg-73) During this period, Milan was rapidly rebuilt, with the construction of several innovative and modernist skyscrapers, such as the [Torre Velasca](/wiki/Torre_Velasca "Torre Velasca") and the [Pirelli Tower](/wiki/Pirelli_Tower "Pirelli Tower"), that soon became the symbols of this new era of prosperity.[[72]](#cite_note-Foot-74) The economic prosperity was, however, overshadowed in the late 1960s and early 1970s during the so-called [Years of lead](/wiki/Years_of_Lead_(Italy) "Years of Lead (Italy)"), when Milan witnessed an unprecedented wave of street violence, [labour strikes](/wiki/Labour_strike "Labour strike") and [political terrorism](/wiki/Political_terrorism "Political terrorism"). The apex of this period of turmoil occurred on 12 December 1969, when [a bomb](/wiki/Piazza_Fontana_bombing "Piazza Fontana bombing") exploded at the National Agrarian Bank in Piazza Fontana, killing 17 people and injuring 88.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/ACTLD_Tree_Of_Life_Expo_2015_Italy_HR_13.jpg/250px-ACTLD_Tree_Of_Life_Expo_2015_Italy_HR_13.jpg)](/wiki/File:ACTLD_Tree_Of_Life_Expo_2015_Italy_HR_13.jpg)

The Tree of Life at the [Expo 2015](/wiki/Expo_2015 "Expo 2015"), which took place in Milan

In the 1980s, with the international success of Milanese houses (like [Armani](/wiki/Armani "Armani"), [Prada](/wiki/Prada "Prada"), [Versace](/wiki/Versace "Versace"), [Moschino](/wiki/Moschino "Moschino") and [Dolce & Gabbana](/wiki/Dolce_%26_Gabbana "Dolce & Gabbana")), Milan became one of the world's fashion capitals. The city saw also a marked rise in [international tourism](/wiki/International_tourism "International tourism"), notably from America and Japan, while the stock exchange increased its market capitalisation more than five-fold.[[73]](#cite_note-75) This period led the mass media to nickname the metropolis *"Milano da bere"*, literally "Milan to be drunk".[[74]](#cite_note-76)

In the 1990s, Milan was badly affected by [Tangentopoli](/wiki/Tangentopoli "Tangentopoli"), a political scandal in which many politicians and businessmen were tried for corruption. The city was also affected by a severe financial crisis and a steady decline in textiles, automobile and steel production.[[72]](#cite_note-Foot-74) Berlusconi's Milano 2 and Milano 3 projects were the most important housing projects of the 1980s and 1990s in Milan and brought to the city new economical and social energy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/3539_-_Milano_-_Via_Dante_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto%2C_6-Mar-2008.jpg/250px-3539_-_Milano_-_Via_Dante_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto%2C_6-Mar-2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:3539_-_Milano_-_Via_Dante_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto,_6-Mar-2008.jpg)

[Via Dante](/wiki/Via_Dante "Via Dante") in Milan, located between [Piazza Cordusio](/wiki/Piazza_Cordusio "Piazza Cordusio") and the [Sforza Castle](/wiki/Sforza_Castle "Sforza Castle")

In the early 21st, century Milan underwent a series of sweeping redevelopments over huge former industrial areas.[[75]](#cite_note-77) Two new business districts, [Porta Nuova](/wiki/Porta_Nuova_(Milan) "Porta Nuova (Milan)") and [CityLife](/wiki/CityLife_(Milan) "CityLife (Milan)"), were built in the space of a decade, radically changing the skyline of the city. Its exhibition centre moved to a much larger site in [Rho](/wiki/Rho,_Lombardy "Rho, Lombardy").[[76]](#cite_note-78) The long decline in traditional manufacturing has been overshadowed by a great expansion of publishing, finance, banking, fashion design, information technology, logistics and tourism.[[77]](#cite_note-79) The city's decades-long population decline seems to have partially reverted in recent years, as the *comune* gained about 100,000 new residents since the last census. The successful re-branding of the city as a global capital of innovation has been instrumental in its successful bids for hosting large international events such as [2015 Expo](/wiki/2015_Expo "2015 Expo") and [2026 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2026_Winter_Olympics "2026 Winter Olympics").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

### Topography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Topography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Milan_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-07-25.jpg/250px-Milan_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-07-25.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milan_by_Sentinel-2,_2020-07-25.jpg)

The northward [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl") since the mid-20th century is visible in this satellite image of Milan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Naviglio_Grande%2C_Milano.jpg/250px-Naviglio_Grande%2C_Milano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Naviglio_Grande,_Milano.jpg)

[Navigli](/wiki/Navigli "Navigli"), a system of interconnected canals in and around Milan, dating back to the Middle Ages

Milan is located in the north-western section of the [Po Valley](/wiki/Po_Valley "Po Valley"), approximately halfway between the river [Po](/wiki/Po_(river) "Po (river)") to the south and the foothills of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") with the great lakes ([Como](/wiki/Lake_Como "Lake Como"), [Maggiore](/wiki/Lake_Maggiore "Lake Maggiore"), and [Lugano](/wiki/Lake_Lugano "Lake Lugano")) to the north, the [Ticino](/wiki/Ticino_(river) "Ticino (river)") river to the west and the [Adda](/wiki/Adda_(river) "Adda (river)") to the east. The city's land is flat and the [elevation](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation") is relatively low; its highest point is 122 m (400 ft) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level").

The administrative *comune*, or city proper, covers an area of about 181 square kilometres (70 sq mi), with a population, in 2025, of 1,366,155 and a population density of 7,520 inhabitants per square kilometre (19,500/sq mi). Milan's continuous urban area extends beyond the city limits. The administrative [Metropolitan City of Milan](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Milan "Metropolitan City of Milan"), a special-status [provincial](/wiki/Provinces_of_Italy "Provinces of Italy") authority, covers 1,575 km2 (608 sq mi) and in 2024 had a population estimated at 3,245,459, with a resulting density of 2,067/km2 (5,350/sq mi),[[78]](#cite_note-Annuario_2025._I_dati_dell’area_metropolitana_di_Milano-80) while its wider metropolitan had an estimated population in excess of 6.1 million as of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit).[[3]](#cite_note-CityPopulation.de-3)

The concentric layout of the city centre reflects the *Navigli*, an ancient system of navigable and interconnected canals, now mostly covered.[[79]](#cite_note-81) The suburbs of the city have expanded mainly to the north, swallowing up many *comuni* along the roads towards Varese, Como, Lecco, and Bergamo.[[80]](#cite_note-82) In the 21st century, the Navigli region of Milan is a highly active area with a large number of residential units, bars, and restaurants; it is also a well-known centre for artists.[[81]](#cite_note-83)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Milano_Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie_ogrod.jpg/250px-Milano_Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie_ogrod.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milano_Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie_ogrod.jpg)

The cloister of [Santa Maria delle Grazie](/wiki/Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie,_Milan "Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan") in the spring of 2010

Milan features a mid-latitude, four-season [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa*), according to the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"). Milan's climate is similar to much of Northern Italy's inland plains, with hot, humid summers and cold, foggy winters. The Alps and [Apennine Mountains](/wiki/Apennine_Mountains "Apennine Mountains") form a natural barrier that protects the city from the major circulations coming from northern Europe and the sea.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

During winter daily average temperatures can fall below freezing (0 °C [32 °F]) and accumulations of snow can occur: the historic average of Milan's area is 25 centimetres (10 in) in the period between 1961 and 1990, with a record of 90 centimetres (35 in) in January 1985. In the suburbs the average can reach 36 centimetres (14 in).[[83]](#cite_note-85) The city receives on average seven days of snow per year.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

The city was often shrouded in thick cloud or fog during winter, although the removal of rice paddies from the southern neighbourhoods and the [urban heat island](/wiki/Urban_heat_island "Urban heat island") effect have greatly reduced this occurrence since the turn of the 21st century. Occasionally, the [Foehn](/wiki/Foehn "Foehn") winds cause the temperatures to rise unexpectedly: on 22 January 2012 the daily high reached 16 °C (61 °F) while on 22 February 2012 it reached 21 °C (70 °F).[[85]](#cite_note-87) Air pollution levels rise significantly in wintertime when [cold air clings to the soil](/wiki/Thermal_inversion "Thermal inversion"), causing Milan to be one of Europe's most polluted cities.[[86]](#cite_note-88)[[87]](#cite_note-89)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Precipitation_in_Milan_from_1940_to_2023.jpg/250px-Precipitation_in_Milan_from_1940_to_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Precipitation_in_Milan_from_1940_to_2023.jpg)

The monthly precipitation in Milan from 1940 to 2023. Data from open-meteo.com

Summers in Milan are hot and humidity levels are high with peak temperatures reaching above 35 °C (95 °F). Due to the high humidity, urban heat effect and lack of wind, nighttimes often remain muggy during the summer months.[[88]](#cite_note-90) Usually the summer enjoys clearer skies with an average of more than 13 hours of daylight:[[89]](#cite_note-WeatherSpark-91) when precipitation occurs though, it is more likely to be accompanied by thunderstorms and [hail](/wiki/Hail "Hail").[[89]](#cite_note-WeatherSpark-91)

Springs and autumns are generally pleasant, with temperatures ranging between 10 and 20 °C (50 and 68 °F); these seasons are characterized by higher rainfall, especially in April and May.[[90]](#cite_note-92) Relative humidity typically ranges between 45% (comfortable) and 95% (very humid) throughout the year, rarely dropping below 27% (dry) and reaching as high as 100%.[[89]](#cite_note-WeatherSpark-91)

Wind is generally absent: over the course of the year typical wind speeds vary from 0 to 14 km/h (0 to 9 mph) (calm to gentle breeze), rarely exceeding 29 km/h (18 mph) (fresh breeze), except during summer thunderstorms when winds can blow strong. In the spring, gale-force windstorms may happen, generated either by [Tramontane](/wiki/Tramontane "Tramontane") blowing from the Alps or by [Bora](/wiki/Bora_(wind) "Bora (wind)")-like winds from the north. Due to its geographic location surrounded by mountains on 3 sides, Milan is among the least windy cities in Europe.[[89]](#cite_note-WeatherSpark-91)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Milan%2C_administrative_divisions_-_Nmbrs_-_colored.svg/250px-Milan%2C_administrative_divisions_-_Nmbrs_-_colored.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Milan,_administrative_divisions_-_Nmbrs_-_colored.svg)

The city's nine *municipi* ("boroughs")

The legislative body of the Italian *comuni* is the [City Council](/wiki/City_Council_of_Milan "City Council of Milan") (*Consiglio Comunale*), which in cities with more than one million population is composed by 48 councillors elected every five years with a proportional system, at the same time of the mayoral elections. The executive body is the City Committee (*Giunta Comunale*), composed by 12 [assessors](/wiki/Assessor_(Italy) "Assessor (Italy)"), that is nominated and presided over by a directly elected [Mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_Milan "Mayor of Milan"). The current mayor of Milan is [Giuseppe Sala](/wiki/Giuseppe_Sala_(politician) "Giuseppe Sala (politician)"), an independent leading a centre-left alliance led by the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(Italy) "Democratic Party (Italy)").

The municipality of Milan is subdivided into nine administrative Borough Councils (*Consigli di Municipio*), down from the former twenty districts before the 1999 administrative reform.[[96]](#cite_note-98) Each Borough Council is governed by a Council (*Consiglio*) and a President, elected contextually to the city Mayor. The urban organisation is governed by the Italian Constitution (art. 114), the Municipal Statute[[97]](#cite_note-99) and several laws, notably the Legislative Decree 267/2000 or Unified Text on Local Administration (*Testo Unico degli Enti Locali*).[[98]](#cite_note-100) After the 2016 administrative reform, the Borough Councils have the power to advise the Mayor with nonbinding opinions on a large spectrum of topics and are responsible for running most local services, such as schools, social services, waste collection, roads, parks, libraries and local commerce; in addition they are supplied with an autonomous funding to finance local activities.

### Metropolitan city

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Metropolitan city")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Milano_-_palazzo_Isimbardi_-_facciata.jpg/250px-Milano_-_palazzo_Isimbardi_-_facciata.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milano_-_palazzo_Isimbardi_-_facciata.jpg)

[Palazzo Isimbardi](/wiki/Palazzo_Isimbardi "Palazzo Isimbardi") is the seat of the Council of the [Metropolitan City of Milan](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Milan "Metropolitan City of Milan").

Milan is the capital of the eponymous Metropolitan city. According to the last governmental dispositions concerning administrative reorganisation, the urban area of Milan is one of the 15 [Metropolitan municipalities](/wiki/Metropolitan_cities_of_Italy "Metropolitan cities of Italy") (*città metropolitane*), new administrative bodies fully operative since 1 January 2015.[[99]](#cite_note-101) The new Metro municipalities, giving large urban areas the administrative powers of a province, are conceived for improving the performance of local administrations and to slash local spending by better co-ordinating the municipalities in providing basic services (including transport, school and social programs) and environment protection.[[100]](#cite_note-Ferri-102) In this policy framework, the Mayor of Milan is designated to exercise the functions of Metropolitan mayor (*Sindaco metropolitano*), presiding over a Metropolitan Council formed by 24 mayors of municipalities within the Metro municipality. The Metropolitan City of Milan is headed by the Metropolitan Mayor (*Sindaco metropolitano*) and by the Metropolitan Council (*Consiglio metropolitano*). Since 21 June 2016, Giuseppe Sala, as mayor of the capital city, has been the mayor of the Metropolitan City.

### Regional government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Regional government")]

Milan is also the capital of Lombardy, one of the twenty [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy") of Italy. Lombardy is by far the most populated region of Italy, with more than ten million inhabitants, almost one sixth of the national total. It is governed by a Regional Council, composed of 80 members elected for a five-year term. On 26 March 2018, a list of candidates of the [centre-right coalition](/wiki/Centre-right_coalition_(Italy) "Centre-right coalition (Italy)"), a coalition of centrist and right-wing parties, led by Attilio Fontana, largely won the regional election, defeating a coalition of socialists, liberals and ecologists and a third-party candidate from the populist [Five Stars Movement](/wiki/Five_Stars_Movement "Five Stars Movement"). The conservatives have governed the region almost uninterruptedly since 1970. The regional council has 48 members from the centre-right coalition, 18 from the [centre-left coalition](/wiki/Centre-left_coalition_(Italy) "Centre-left coalition (Italy)") and 13 from the Five Star Movement. The seat of the regional government is [Palazzo Lombardia](/wiki/Palazzo_Lombardia "Palazzo Lombardia") that, standing at 161.3 metres (529 feet),[[101]](#cite_note-103) is the fifth-tallest building in Milan.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/20161207_Palazzo_Castiglioni.jpg/250px-20161207_Palazzo_Castiglioni.jpg)](/wiki/File:20161207_Palazzo_Castiglioni.jpg)

[Palazzo Castiglioni](/wiki/Palazzo_Castiglioni_(Milan) "Palazzo Castiglioni (Milan)"), designed in the [Liberty style](/wiki/Liberty_style "Liberty style"), on [Corso Venezia](/wiki/Corso_Venezia "Corso Venezia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Palais_Banca_Commerciale_Italiana_-_Milan_%28IT25%29_-_2022-09-02_-_4.jpg/250px-Palais_Banca_Commerciale_Italiana_-_Milan_%28IT25%29_-_2022-09-02_-_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palais_Banca_Commerciale_Italiana_-_Milan_(IT25)_-_2022-09-02_-_4.jpg)

The historic [Banca Commerciale Italiana](/wiki/Banca_Commerciale_Italiana "Banca Commerciale Italiana") headquarters at the corner of [Piazza della Scala](/wiki/Piazza_della_Scala "Piazza della Scala") and [Via Manzoni](/wiki/Via_Manzoni "Via Manzoni")

The [architectural and artistic presence in Milan](/wiki/History_of_architecture_and_art_in_Milan "History of architecture and art in Milan") represents one of the attractions of the Lombard capital. Milan has been among the most important Italian centers in the [history of architecture](/wiki/History_of_architecture "History of architecture"), has made important contributions to the development of art history, and has been the cradle of a number of [modern art](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art") movements.

There are only few remains of the ancient Roman city, notably the well-preserved [Colonne di San Lorenzo](/wiki/Colonne_di_San_Lorenzo "Colonne di San Lorenzo"). During the second half of the 4th century, Saint Ambrose, as bishop of Milan, had a strong influence on the layout of the city, reshaping the centre (although the cathedral and baptistery built in Roman times are now lost) and building the great basilicas at the city gates: Sant'Ambrogio, [San Nazaro in Brolo](/wiki/San_Nazaro_in_Brolo "San Nazaro in Brolo"), [San Simpliciano](/wiki/Basilica_of_San_Simpliciano "Basilica of San Simpliciano") and [Sant'Eustorgio](/wiki/Basilica_of_Sant%27Eustorgio "Basilica of Sant'Eustorgio"), which still stand, refurbished over the centuries, as some of the finest and most important churches in Milan. [Milan's Cathedral](/wiki/Duomo_di_Milano "Duomo di Milano"), built between 1386 and 1877, is the largest church in the Italian Republic—the larger [St. Peter's Basilica](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Basilica "St. Peter's Basilica") is in the [State of Vatican City](/wiki/State_of_Vatican_City "State of Vatican City"), a sovereign state—and the [third largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_churches "List of largest churches"),[[102]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-104) as well as the most important example of [Gothic architecture](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") in Italy. The gilt bronze statue of the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Madonnina_(statue) "Madonnina (statue)"), placed in 1774 on the highest pinnacle of the Duomo, soon became one of the most enduring symbols of Milan.[[104]](#cite_note-106)

In the 15th century, when the Sforza ruled the city, an old Viscontean fortress was enlarged and embellished to become the Castello Sforzesco, the seat of an elegant Renaissance court surrounded by a walled hunting park. Notable architects involved in the project included the [Florentine](/wiki/Florence "Florence") [Filarete](/wiki/Filarete "Filarete"), who was commissioned to build the high central entrance tower, and the military specialist Bartolomeo Gadio.[[105]](#cite_note-107) The alliance between Francesco Sforza and Florence's [Cosimo de' Medici](/wiki/Cosimo_de%27_Medici "Cosimo de' Medici") bore to Milan Tuscan models of Renaissance architecture, apparent in the [Ospedale Maggiore](/wiki/Ospedale_Maggiore "Ospedale Maggiore") and Bramante's work in the city, which includes Santa Maria presso San Satiro (a reconstruction of a small 9th-century church), the tribune of [Santa Maria delle Grazie](/wiki/Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie_(Milan) "Santa Maria delle Grazie (Milan)") and three cloisters for Sant'Ambrogio.[[106]](#cite_note-murray-108) The [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation") in the 16th to 17th centuries was also the period of [Spanish domination](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") and was marked by two powerful figures: [Saint Charles Borromeo](/wiki/Charles_Borromeo "Charles Borromeo") and his cousin, [Cardinal Federico Borromeo](/wiki/Federico_Borromeo "Federico Borromeo"). Not only did they impose themselves as moral guides to the people of Milan, but they also gave a great impulse to culture, with the creation of the [Biblioteca Ambrosiana](/wiki/Biblioteca_Ambrosiana "Biblioteca Ambrosiana"), in a building designed by [Francesco Maria Richini](/wiki/Francesco_Maria_Richini "Francesco Maria Richini"), and the nearby Pinacoteca Ambrosiana. Many notable churches and Baroque mansions were built in the city during this period by the architects [Pellegrino Tibaldi](/wiki/Pellegrino_Tibaldi "Pellegrino Tibaldi"), [Galeazzo Alessi](/wiki/Galeazzo_Alessi "Galeazzo Alessi") and Richini himself.
[[107]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-109) Empress [Maria Theresa of Austria](/wiki/Maria_Theresa_of_Austria "Maria Theresa of Austria") was responsible for the significant renovations carried out in Milan during the 18th century.[[108]](#cite_note-110) This urban and artistic renewal included the establishment of [Teatro alla Scala](/wiki/La_Scala "La Scala"), inaugurated in 1778, and the renovation of the Royal Palace. The late 1700s [Palazzo Belgioioso](/wiki/Palazzo_Belgioioso "Palazzo Belgioioso") by [Giuseppe Piermarini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Piermarini "Giuseppe Piermarini") and [Royal Villa of Milan](/wiki/Royal_Villa_of_Milan "Royal Villa of Milan") by [Leopoldo Pollack](/wiki/Leopoldo_Pollack "Leopoldo Pollack"), later the official residence of Austrian viceroys, are often regarded among the best examples of [Neoclassical architecture](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") in Lombardy.[[109]](#cite_note-111) From 1805 to 1814, during the [Napoleonic](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") rule of the city, Milan was established as the capital of a satellite Kingdom of Italy and steps were taken to reshape it in accordince with its newly acquired status. This included construction of large boulevards, new squares ([Porta Ticinese](/wiki/Porta_Ticinese "Porta Ticinese") by [Luigi Cagnola](/wiki/Luigi_Cagnola "Luigi Cagnola") and Foro Bonaparte by [Giovanni Antonio Antolini](/wiki/Giovanni_Antonio_Antolini "Giovanni Antonio Antolini")), and cultural institutions ([Art Gallery](/wiki/Pinacoteca_di_Brera "Pinacoteca di Brera") and the [Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/Brera_Academy "Brera Academy")).[[110]](#cite_note-112) The massive [Arch of Peace](/wiki/Arch_of_Peace "Arch of Peace"), situated at the bottom of Corso Sempione, is often compared to the [Arc de Triomphe](/wiki/Arc_de_Triomphe "Arc de Triomphe") in Paris. In the second half of the 19th century, Milan quickly became the main industrial centre of the new Italian nation, drawing inspiration from the great European capitals that were hubs of the [Second Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Second_Industrial_Revolution "Second Industrial Revolution"). The great [Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II](/wiki/Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II "Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II"), realised by [Giuseppe Mengoni](/wiki/Giuseppe_Mengoni "Giuseppe Mengoni") between 1865 and 1877 to celebrate [Vittorio Emanuele II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel II of Italy"), is a covered passage with a glass and cast iron roof, inspired by the [Burlington Arcade](/wiki/Burlington_Arcade "Burlington Arcade") in London. Several other arcades such as the [Galleria del Corso](/wiki/Galleria_del_Corso "Galleria del Corso"), built between 1923 and 1931, complement it. Another late-19th-century eclectic monument in the city is the [Cimitero Monumentale](/wiki/Cimitero_Monumentale "Cimitero Monumentale") graveyard, built in a [Neo-Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_Revival_architecture "Romanesque Revival architecture") style between 1863 and 1866.

The tumultuous period of early 20th century brought several, radical innovations in Milanese architecture. [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau"), also known as *[Liberty](/wiki/Liberty_style "Liberty style")* in Italy, is recognisable in [Palazzo Castiglioni](/wiki/Palazzo_Castiglioni_(Milan) "Palazzo Castiglioni (Milan)"), built by architect [Giuseppe Sommaruga](/wiki/Giuseppe_Sommaruga "Giuseppe Sommaruga") between 1901 and 1903.[[107]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-109) Other examples include Hotel Corso,[[107]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-109) [Casa Guazzoni](/wiki/Casa_Guazzoni "Casa Guazzoni") with its wrought iron and staircase, and Berri-Meregalli house, the latter built in a traditional Milanese Art Nouveau style combined with elements of neo-Romanesque and Gothic revival architecture, regarded as one of the last such types of architecture in the city.[[111]](#cite_note-113) A new, more eclectic form of architecture can be seen in buildings such as Castello Cova, built the 1910s in a distinctly neo-medieval style, evoking the architectural trends of the past.[[112]](#cite_note-114) An important example of [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco"), which blended such styles with [Fascist architecture](/wiki/Fascist_architecture "Fascist architecture"), is the huge [Central railway station](/wiki/Milano_Centrale "Milano Centrale") inaugurated in 1931.[[113]](#cite_note-115)

The post-World War II period saw rapid reconstruction and fast economic growth, accompanied by a nearly two-fold increase in population. In the 1950s and 1960s, a strong demand for new residential and commercial areas drove to extreme urban expansion, that has produced some of the major milestones in the city's architectural history, including [Gio Ponti](/wiki/Gi%C3%B2_Ponti "Giò Ponti")'s Pirelli Tower (1956–60), Velasca Tower (1956–58), and the creation of brand new residential satellite towns, as well as huge amounts of low-quality public housings. In recent years, de-industrialization, [urban decay](/wiki/Urban_decay "Urban decay") and gentrification led to a vast urban renewal of former industrial areas, that have been transformed into modern residential and financial districts, notably Porta Nuova in downtown Milan and [FieraMilano](/wiki/FieraMilano "FieraMilano") in the suburb of [Rho](/wiki/Rho_(Italy) "Rho (Italy)"). In addition, the old exhibition area is being completely reshaped according to the Citylife regeneration project, featuring residencial areas, museums, an urban park and three skyscrapers designed by international architects, and after whom they are named: the 202-metre (663-foot) [Isozaki Arata](/wiki/Isozaki_Arata "Isozaki Arata") (when completed, the tallest building in Italy),[[114]](#cite_note-Isozaki_Tower_Citylife-116) the twisted [Hadid Tower](/wiki/Zaha_Hadid "Zaha Hadid"),[[115]](#cite_note-Hadid_Tower_Citylife-117) and the curved [Libeskind Tower](/wiki/Daniel_Libeskind "Daniel Libeskind").[[116]](#cite_note-Liebskind_Tower_Citylife-118)

Two business districts dominate Milan's skyline: *Porta Nuova* in the north-east (boroughs No. 9 and 2) and *CityLife* (borough No. 8) in the north-west part of the commune. The [tallest buildings](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Milan "List of tallest buildings in Milan") include the [Unicredit Tower](/wiki/Unicredit_Tower "Unicredit Tower") at 231 m (though only 162 m without the spire), and the 209 m [Allianz Tower](/wiki/Allianz_Tower "Allianz Tower"), a 50-story tower.

### Parks and gardens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Parks and gardens")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Laghetto_nel_Parco_Sempione_con_tartarughe%2C_sullo_sfondo_il_Castello_Sforzesco.jpg/250px-Laghetto_nel_Parco_Sempione_con_tartarughe%2C_sullo_sfondo_il_Castello_Sforzesco.jpg)](/wiki/File:Laghetto_nel_Parco_Sempione_con_tartarughe,_sullo_sfondo_il_Castello_Sforzesco.jpg)

[Sempione Park](/wiki/Parco_Sempione "Parco Sempione")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Giardini_pubblici_Indro_Montanelli.jpg/250px-Giardini_pubblici_Indro_Montanelli.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giardini_pubblici_Indro_Montanelli.jpg)

[Montanelli Gardens](/wiki/Giardini_Pubblici_Indro_Montanelli "Giardini Pubblici Indro Montanelli")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Brera_Botanical_Garden_-_4.jpg/250px-Brera_Botanical_Garden_-_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brera_Botanical_Garden_-_4.jpg)

[Orto Botanico di Brera](/wiki/Orto_Botanico_di_Brera "Orto Botanico di Brera")

The largest parks in the central area of Milan are [Sempione Park](/wiki/Parco_Sempione "Parco Sempione"), at the north-western edge, and [Montanelli Gardens](/wiki/Giardini_Pubblici_Indro_Montanelli "Giardini Pubblici Indro Montanelli"), situated north-east of the city. English-style Sempione Park, built in 1890, contains the [Civic Arena](/wiki/Arena_Civica "Arena Civica"), the [Civic Aquarium of Milan](/wiki/Civic_Aquarium_of_Milan "Civic Aquarium of Milan") (which is the third oldest aquarium in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe")[[117]](#cite_note-119)), a steel lattice panoramic tower, an art exhibition centre, a Japanese garden and a public library.[[118]](#cite_note-120) The Montanelli gardens, created in the 18th century, hosts the [Natural History Museum of Milan](/wiki/Museo_Civico_di_Storia_Naturale_di_Milano "Museo Civico di Storia Naturale di Milano") and a [planetarium](/wiki/Planetario_di_Milano "Planetario di Milano").[[119]](#cite_note-121) Slightly away from the city centre, heading east, Forlanini Park is characterised by a large pond and a few preserved shacks which remind of the area's agricultural past.[[120]](#cite_note-122) In recent years Milan's authorities pledged to develop its green areas: they planned to create twenty new urban parks and extend the already existing ones, and announced plans to plant three million trees by 2030.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Also notable is [Monte Stella](/wiki/Monte_Stella_(Milan) "Monte Stella (Milan)") ("Starmount"), also informally called Montagnetta di San Siro ("Little mountain of San Siro"), an artificial hill and surrounding city park in Milan. The hill was created using the debris from the buildings that were bombed during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), as well as from the last remnants of the [Spanish walls](/wiki/Walls_of_Milan "Walls of Milan") of the city, demolished in the mid 20th century. Even at only 25 m (82 ft) height, the hill provides a panoramic view of the city and hinterland, and in a clear day, the Alps and Apennines can be distinguished from atop. A notable area of the park is called "Giardino dei Giusti" (Garden of the Just), which is a memorial to distinguished opponents of genocide and crimes against humanity; each tree in the garden is dedicated to one such person. Notable people who have been dedicated a tree in the Giardino dei Giusti include [Moshe Bejski](/wiki/Moshe_Bejski "Moshe Bejski"), [Andrej Sakharov](/wiki/Andrej_Sakharov "Andrej Sakharov"), [Svetlana Broz](/wiki/Svetlana_Broz "Svetlana Broz"), and [Pietro Kuciukian](/wiki/Pietro_Kuciukian "Pietro Kuciukian").

The [Orto Botanico di Brera](/wiki/Orto_Botanico_di_Brera "Orto Botanico di Brera") a [botanical garden](/wiki/Botanical_garden "Botanical garden") located behind [Palazzo Brera](/wiki/Palazzo_Brera "Palazzo Brera") at Via Brera 28 in the center of Milan, is another major park in the city. The garden consists primarily of rectangular flower-beds, trimmed in brick, with elliptical ponds from the 18th century, and specula and [greenhouse](/wiki/Greenhouse "Greenhouse") from the 19th century (now used by the Academy of Fine Arts). It contains one of the oldest *[Ginkgo biloba](/wiki/Ginkgo_biloba "Ginkgo biloba")* trees in Europe, as well as mature specimens of *[Firmiana platanifolia](/wiki/Firmiana_platanifolia "Firmiana platanifolia")*, *[Juglans nigra](/wiki/Juglans_nigra "Juglans nigra")*, *[Pterocarya fraxinifolia](/wiki/Pterocarya_fraxinifolia "Pterocarya fraxinifolia")*, and *[Tilia](/wiki/Tilia "Tilia")*.

In addition, even though Milan is located in one of the most urbanised regions of Italy, it is surrounded by a belt of green areas and features numerous gardens even in its very centre. The farmlands and woodlands north (Parco Nord Milano since 1975) and south ([Parco Agricolo Sud Milano](/wiki/Parco_Agricolo_Sud_Milano "Parco Agricolo Sud Milano") since 1990) of the urban area have been protected as regional parks.[[121]](#cite_note-123)[[122]](#cite_note-124) West of the city, the Parco delle Cave (Sand pit park) has been established on a neglected site where gravel and sand used to be extracted, featuring artificial lakes and woods.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit), 1,366,155 people live in Milan's city proper, while 3,247,623 people live in the province-level municipality. The population of Milan today is lower than its historical peak. With rapid industrialization in post-war years, the population of Milan peaked at 1,743,427 in 1973.[[126]](#cite_note-128) Thereafter, during the following decades, about one third of the population moved to the outer belt of suburbs and new satellite settlements that grew around the city proper.

Today, Milan's conurbation extends well beyond the borders of the city proper and of its special-status provincial authority: its contiguous built-up urban area was home to 5.27 million people in 2015,[[127]](#cite_note-Demographia-129) while its wider [metropolitan area](/wiki/Milan_metropolitan_area "Milan metropolitan area"), the largest in Italy and fourth largest in the EU, is estimated to have a population in excess of 6.1 million as of 2025.[[3]](#cite_note-CityPopulation.de-3)
