As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit), some 269,397 foreign residents lived in the municipality of Milan, representing 19.6% of the total resident population.[[129]](#cite_note-:1-131) These figures suggest that the immigrant population has more than doubled in the last 15 years.[[130]](#cite_note-municipalitystat03-132)

After World War II, Milan experienced two main waves of immigration: the first, dating from the 1950s to the early 1970s, saw a large influx of migrants from poorer and rural areas within Italy; the second, starting from the late 1980s, has been characterized by the preponderance of foreign-born immigrants.[[131]](#cite_note-133)

The early period coincided with the so-called Italian economic miracle of postwar years, an era of extraordinary growth based on rapid industrial expansion and great public works, that brought to the city a large influx of over 400,000 people, mainly from rural and underdeveloped Southern Italy.[[72]](#cite_note-Foot-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Lazzaretto_di_Milano_da_via_San_Gregorio_01.JPG/250px-Lazzaretto_di_Milano_da_via_San_Gregorio_01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lazzaretto_di_Milano_da_via_San_Gregorio_01.JPG)

Russian church in Milan

Decades of continuing high immigration have made the city one of the most cosmopolitan and multicultural in Italy. Immigrants came mainly from Africa (in particular [Eritreans](/wiki/Eritreans_in_Italy "Eritreans in Italy"), [Egyptians](/wiki/Egyptians_in_Italy "Egyptians in Italy"), [Moroccans](/wiki/Moroccans_in_Italy "Moroccans in Italy"), [Senegalese](/wiki/Senegalese_people_in_Italy "Senegalese people in Italy") and [Nigerian](/wiki/Nigerian_people_in_Italy "Nigerian people in Italy")), and the former socialist countries of Eastern Europe (notably [Albanians](/wiki/Albanians_in_Italy "Albanians in Italy"), [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians_in_Italy "Romanians in Italy"), [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians_in_Italy "Ukrainians in Italy"), [Macedonians](/wiki/Macedonian_diaspora "Macedonian diaspora"), [Moldovans](/wiki/Moldovans "Moldovans"), and [Russians](/w/index.php?title=Russians_in_italy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Russians in italy (page does not exist)")), in addition to a growing number of Asians (in particular [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_people_in_Italy "Chinese people in Italy"), [Sri Lankans](/wiki/Sri_Lankans_in_Italy "Sri Lankans in Italy") and [Filipinos](/wiki/Filipino_Italians "Filipino Italians")) and Latin Americans (Mainly South Americans). At the beginning of the 1990s, Milan already had a population of foreign-born residents of approximately 58,000 (or 4% of the then population), that rose rapidly to over 117,000 by the end of the decade (about 9% of the total).[[132]](#cite_note-134)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Milano_-_Quartiere_cinese_nel_1945.jpg/250px-Milano_-_Quartiere_cinese_nel_1945.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milano_-_Quartiere_cinese_nel_1945.jpg)

Milan Chinatown in 1945

Milan is home to the second-largest [Far East Asian](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") community in Europe after [Paris](/wiki/Vietnamese_community_in_Paris "Vietnamese community in Paris"), with the Philippines and China, making up about a quarter of its foreign population (around 76,000 out of 301,000 in 2023). Another 4,000 foreigners come from other East Asian countries; notably, Milan hosts more than 2,000 Japanese nationals and 1,000 Koreans, excluding those who also hold Italian citizenship.[[133]](#cite_note-135)[[129]](#cite_note-:1-131) Foreigners holding East-Asian citizenship thus make up around 5.36% of the city's population. Milan notably hosts the oldest and largest (along with [Prato](/wiki/Prato "Prato")) [Chinese community](/wiki/Chinatown,_Milan "Chinatown, Milan") in Italy, with around 37,000 people in 2023, excluding Italians of Chinese descent such as immigrants who have acquired Italian citizenship or their descendants. Situated in the [8th district](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Milan "Administrative divisions of Milan"), and centered on [Via Paolo Sarpi](/wiki/Via_Paolo_Sarpi "Via Paolo Sarpi"), an important commercial avenue, the Milanese Chinatown was originally established in the 1920s by immigrants from [Wencheng County](/wiki/Wencheng_County "Wencheng County"), in the [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"), and used to operate small textile and leather workshops.[[134]](#cite_note-136) Milan also hosts a Japanese International school as well as various Chinese schools throughout the city.[[135]](#cite_note-137)[[136]](#cite_note-138)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Milano_-_incrocio_via_Settala_via_Vitruvio_-_01.jpg/250px-Milano_-_incrocio_via_Settala_via_Vitruvio_-_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milano_-_incrocio_via_Settala_via_Vitruvio_-_01.jpg)

Via Settala, one of the access points to the so-called "Asmarina" area

The city also hosts an historical African community originating from the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa"). As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit), there were around 4,000 Eritrean, Ethiopian or Somali-born people living in Milan, the overwhelming majority being double-citizens of Italy, and not counting second and third generation migrants. The three countries were all Italian colonies at a time, from 1869 ([Eritrea](/wiki/Italian_Eritrea "Italian Eritrea"))[[137]](#cite_note-EBAb-139)[[138]](#cite_note-140)[[139]](#cite_note-141) to 1943 ([East African campaign](/wiki/East_African_campaign_(World_War_II) "East African campaign (World War II)")). Due to the historical links with Italy, a small community originating from the Horn of Africa has established its presence near Porta Venezia district starting from the 1970s.[[140]](#cite_note-142)[[141]](#cite_note-143)[[142]](#cite_note-144)[[143]](#cite_note-145) It is estimated that in the "Asmarina" area (Little Asmara) there are around 2,000-2,500 people from the Horn of Africa still living there, along with multiple restaurants, institutes as well as an Ethiopian Church.[[144]](#cite_note-146)[[145]](#cite_note-147)[[146]](#cite_note-148)

Another notable area with a large presence of foreign residents coming from a specific country is the so-called "quadrilatero di San Siro" or "San Siro casbah" in reference to the large Arab-speaking population living in the area.[[147]](#cite_note-149)[[148]](#cite_note-150)[[149]](#cite_note-151)[[150]](#cite_note-152) The neighbourhood, consisting of around 6,000 municipal flats, is characterised by the fact of having an estimated 25% share of Arab-speaking inhabitants, mostly hailing from Egypt.[[151]](#cite_note-153)[[152]](#cite_note-154)[[153]](#cite_note-155) The area has often been described as a *[banlieue](/wiki/Banlieue "Banlieue")* within Milan and has historically had a higher crime rate than the rest of the city. Nevertheless, in recent years many projects have been presented so as to mitigate the marginalisation of its inhabitants. Other areas hosting large Arabic-speaking populations include Maciachini-Imbonati, Corvetto, Comasina and piazza Arcole.[[154]](#cite_note-156)[[155]](#cite_note-157)[[156]](#cite_note-158)[[157]](#cite_note-159)[[158]](#cite_note-160)[[159]](#cite_note-161)

Milan has a substantial English-speaking community (around 4,500 US citizens, British, Irish and Australian expatriates, excluding double-citizens), and several English schools and English-language publications, such as *Hello Milano*, *Where Milano* and *[Easy Milano](/wiki/Easy_Milano "Easy Milano")*.[[129]](#cite_note-:1-131)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie_Milan_2013.jpg/250px-Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie_Milan_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie_Milan_2013.jpg)

[Santa Maria delle Grazie](/wiki/Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie,_Milan "Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan"), completed in 1497

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Sant%27Ambrogio_%28Milan%29_-_Atrium.jpg/250px-Sant%27Ambrogio_%28Milan%29_-_Atrium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sant%27Ambrogio_(Milan)_-_Atrium.jpg)

[St. Ambrose Basilica](/wiki/Basilica_di_Sant%27Ambrogio "Basilica di Sant'Ambrogio") dates back to AD 379–386.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Veduta_della_Biblioteca_Ambrosiana%2C_Milano%2C_di_tre_quarti.jpg/250px-Veduta_della_Biblioteca_Ambrosiana%2C_Milano%2C_di_tre_quarti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Veduta_della_Biblioteca_Ambrosiana,_Milano,_di_tre_quarti.jpg)

The [Biblioteca Ambrosiana](/wiki/Biblioteca_Ambrosiana "Biblioteca Ambrosiana") and [Pinacoteca Ambrosiana](/wiki/Biblioteca_Ambrosiana "Biblioteca Ambrosiana") are among the city's most important libraries and art galleries, with a large collection of historic books, manuscripts, sketches and paintings from the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"). The [Pinacoteca di Brera](/wiki/Pinacoteca_di_Brera "Pinacoteca di Brera") is the main art gallery for paintings in Milan.

Milan's population, like that of Italy as a whole, is mostly Catholic.[[160]](#cite_note-162)[[161]](#cite_note-PEW2018-RESEARCH-163) It is the seat of the Archdiocese of Milan. Greater Milan is also home to [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), Jewish, [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim"), [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Sikh](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") and [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") communities.[[162]](#cite_note-164)[[163]](#cite_note-165)[[164]](#cite_note-166)[[165]](#cite_note-167)[[166]](#cite_note-168)

Milan has been a Christian-majority city since the late Roman Empire.[[167]](#cite_note-169) Its religious history was marked by the figure of St. Ambrose, whose heritage includes the [Ambrosian Rite](/wiki/Ambrosian_Rite "Ambrosian Rite") (Italian: *Rito ambrosiano*), used by some five million Catholics in the greater part of the [Archdiocese of Milan](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Milan "Archdiocese of Milan"),[[168]](#cite_note-170) which consider the largest in Europe.[[169]](#cite_note-171) The Rite varies slightly from the canonical [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") [liturgy](/wiki/Catholic_liturgy "Catholic liturgy"), with differences in the mass, liturgical year ([Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") starts four days later than in the Roman Rite), baptism, rite of funerals, priest clothes and sacred music (use of the [Ambrosian chant](/wiki/Ambrosian_chant "Ambrosian chant") rather than Gregorian).[[170]](#cite_note-172)

In addition, the city is home to the largest Orthodox community in Italy. Lombardy is the seat of at least 78 Orthodox parishes and monasteries, the vast majority of them located in the area of Milan.[[171]](#cite_note-173) The main [Romanian Orthodox](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox "Romanian Orthodox") church in Milan is the Catholic church of Our Lady of Victory (Chiesa di Santa Maria della Vittoria), currently granted for use to the local Romanian community.[[172]](#cite_note-174) Similarly, the point of reference for the followers of the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") is the Catholic church of [San Vito in Pasquirolo](/wiki/San_Vito_in_Pasquirolo,_Milan "San Vito in Pasquirolo, Milan").[[173]](#cite_note-175)[[174]](#cite_note-176)

The Jewish community of Milan is the second largest in Italy after Rome, with about 10,000 members, mainly [Sephardi](/wiki/Sephardi "Sephardi").[[175]](#cite_note-177) The main city synagogue, Hechal David u-Mordechai Temple, was built by architect [Luca Beltrami](/wiki/Luca_Beltrami "Luca Beltrami") in 1892 and is also the community's main headquarters, is located in Via della Guastalla. The interior was renovated in 1997.

Milan hosts also one of the largest Muslim communities in Italy,[[176]](#cite_note-178) and the city saw the construction of the country's first new [mosque](/wiki/Mosque_of_Segrate "Mosque of Segrate") featuring a dome and minaret, since the destruction of the ancient mosques of [Lucera](/wiki/Lucera "Lucera") in the year 1300. In 2014 the City Council agreed on the construction of a new mosque amid bitter political debate, since it is strenuously opposed by right-wing parties such as the [Northern League](/wiki/Northern_League_(Italy) "Northern League (Italy)").[[177]](#cite_note-179) As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit), the Muslim population is estimated at 9% of the city's population.[[178]](#cite_note-180)

Currently, accurate statistics on the Hindu and Sikh presence in Milan metro area are not available; however, various sources estimate that about 40% of the total Indian population living in Italy, or about 50,000 individuals, reside in Lombardy,[[179]](#cite_note-181)[[180]](#cite_note-182) where a number of Hindu and Sikh temples exist and where they form the largest such communities in Europe after the ones in Britain.[[181]](#cite_note-183)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Milan](/wiki/Economy_of_Milan "Economy of Milan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Palais_Mezzanotte_-_Milan_%28IT25%29_-_2022-09-03_-_3.jpg/250px-Palais_Mezzanotte_-_Milan_%28IT25%29_-_2022-09-03_-_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palais_Mezzanotte_-_Milan_(IT25)_-_2022-09-03_-_3.jpg)

The skyscrapers of [Porta Nuova business district](/wiki/Porta_Nuova_(Milan) "Porta Nuova (Milan)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Fieramilano_Rho_Pero_improved_version.jpg/250px-Fieramilano_Rho_Pero_improved_version.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fieramilano_Rho_Pero_improved_version.jpg)

[Fiera Milano](/wiki/Fiera_Milano "Fiera Milano"), the most important trade fair organiser in Italy and the world's fourth largest[[182]](#cite_note-Fiera-184)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Via_Monte_Napoleone%2C_Milan%2C_Italy.jpg/250px-Via_Monte_Napoleone%2C_Milan%2C_Italy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Via_Monte_Napoleone,_Milan,_Italy.jpg)

[Via Monte Napoleone](/wiki/Via_Monte_Napoleone "Via Monte Napoleone") was Europe's and the world's most expensive street in 2024,[[183]](#cite_note-185) outranking [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") (the most expensive in 2023),[[184]](#cite_note-186) but was outranked by [New Bond Street](/wiki/New_Bond_Street "New Bond Street") in [London](/wiki/London "London") in 2025.[[185]](#cite_note-Modaes-19.11.2025-187)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Prada_milano.JPG/250px-Prada_milano.JPG)](/wiki/File:Prada_milano.JPG)

[Prada](/wiki/Prada "Prada") shop at [Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II](/wiki/Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II "Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II") in Milan

Whereas Rome is Italy's political and cultural capital, Milan is the country's industrial and financial heart, and it is widely considered the economic capital of Italy[[9]](#cite_note-prologis-11) and one of the wealthiest cities in the country in terms of per-capita GDP.[[186]](#cite_note-188)[[187]](#cite_note-189) According to [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat"), Milan's metropolitan region GDP was €228 billion ($265 billion) in 2024,[[5]](#cite_note-Eurostat_metro_area_GDP_data-5) while the whole Lombardy's [gross regional product](/wiki/Gross_regional_domestic_product "Gross regional domestic product") was €490 billion ($568 billion),[[188]](#cite_note-190) the [second-largest](/wiki/List_of_European_Union_regions_by_GDP "List of European Union regions by GDP") among EU regions.

Milan is a member of the [Blue Banana](/wiki/Blue_Banana "Blue Banana") corridor and of the [Four Motors for Europe](/wiki/Four_Motors_for_Europe "Four Motors for Europe") among Europe's economic leaders. The urban region of Milan is home to about 45% of businesses in the Lombardy region and more than 8 percent of all businesses in Italy, including three [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies.[[189]](#cite_note-191) According to the [Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist_Intelligence_Unit "Economist Intelligence Unit"), Milan was the 11th-most-expensive city in Europe and the 22nd-most-expensive city in the world in 2019.[[190]](#cite_note-192) [Via Monte Napoleone](/wiki/Via_Monte_Napoleone "Via Monte Napoleone") has been defined as the most-expensive street in the world (2024).[[191]](#cite_note-193)

Milan is one of the [fashion capitals](/wiki/Fashion_capital "Fashion capital") of the world, where the sector can count on 12,000 companies, 800 show rooms and 6,000 sales outlets; the city hosts the headquarters of global fashion houses such as Armani, [Bottega Veneta](/wiki/Bottega_Veneta "Bottega Veneta"), [Costume National](/wiki/Costume_National "Costume National"), Dolce & Gabbana, [Dsquared²](/wiki/Dean_and_Dan_Caten "Dean and Dan Caten"), [Etro](/wiki/Etro "Etro"), [Jil Sander](/wiki/Jil_Sander_(brand) "Jil Sander (brand)"), [Loro Piana](/wiki/Loro_Piana "Loro Piana"), [Luxottica](/wiki/Luxottica "Luxottica"), [Marni](/wiki/Marni_(brand) "Marni (brand)"), [Moncler](/wiki/Moncler "Moncler"), Moschino, Prada, Versace, [Valentino](/wiki/Valentino_Garavani "Valentino Garavani"), [Trussardi](/wiki/Trussardi "Trussardi") and [Zegna](/wiki/Zegna "Zegna") and four weeks a year are dedicated to fashion events.[[192]](#cite_note-City_profile-194)

Since the late 1800s, the area of Milan has been a major industrial and manufacturing center. [Alfa Romeo](/wiki/Alfa_Romeo "Alfa Romeo") automobile company and [Falck](/wiki/Falck_Group "Falck Group") steel group employed thousands of workers in the city until the closure of their sites in [Arese](/wiki/Arese "Arese") in 2004 and [Sesto San Giovanni](/wiki/Sesto_San_Giovanni "Sesto San Giovanni") in 1995.

Other global industrial companies, such as [Edison](/wiki/Edison_(company) "Edison (company)"), [Prysmian Group](/wiki/Prysmian_Group "Prysmian Group"), [Riva Group](/wiki/Gruppo_Riva "Gruppo Riva"), [Saras](/wiki/Saras_S.p.A. "Saras S.p.A."), [Saipem](/wiki/Saipem "Saipem"), Luxottica, [Pirelli](/wiki/Pirelli "Pirelli"), [UniCredit](/wiki/UniCredit "UniCredit") and [Techint](/wiki/Techint "Techint"), maintain their headquarters and significant employment in the city and its suburbs. Other relevant industries active in metro Milan include chemicals (e.g. [Mapei](/wiki/Mapei "Mapei"), [Versalis](/wiki/Versalis "Versalis"), [Tamoil Italy](/wiki/Tamoil "Tamoil")), home appliances (e.g. [Candy](/wiki/Candy_(company) "Candy (company)")), hospitality ([UNA Hotels & Resorts](/wiki/UNA_Hotels_%26_Resorts "UNA Hotels & Resorts")), food & beverages (e.g. [Bertolli](/wiki/Bertolli "Bertolli"), [Campari](/wiki/Campari_Group "Campari Group")), machinery, medical technologies (e.g. [Amplifon](/wiki/Amplifon "Amplifon"), [Bracco](/wiki/Bracco_(company) "Bracco (company)")), plastics and textiles. The construction (e.g. [Webuild](/wiki/Webuild "Webuild")), retail (e.g. [Esselunga](/wiki/Esselunga "Esselunga"), [La Rinascente](/wiki/La_Rinascente "La Rinascente")) and utilities (e.g. [A2A](/wiki/A2A "A2A"), Edison S.p.A., [Snam](/wiki/Snam "Snam"), [Sorgenia](/w/index.php?title=Sorgenia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sorgenia (page does not exist)")) sectors are also large employers in the Greater Milan. Other major companies based in Milan include [Fininvest](/wiki/Fininvest "Fininvest"), [TIM Group](/wiki/TIM_Group "TIM Group") and [Banco BPM](/wiki/Banco_BPM "Banco BPM").

The main national insurance companies and banking groups (for a total of 198 companies) and over forty foreign insurance and banking companies are located in the city,[[192]](#cite_note-City_profile-194) as well as a number of asset management companies, including [Anima Holding](/wiki/Anima_Holding "Anima Holding"), [Azimut Holding](/wiki/Azimut_Holding "Azimut Holding"), [ARCA SGR](/wiki/ARCA_SGR "ARCA SGR") and [Eurizon Capital](/wiki/Eurizon_Capital "Eurizon Capital"). The [Associazione Bancaria Italiana](/wiki/Associazione_Bancaria_Italiana "Associazione Bancaria Italiana") representing the Italian banking system, and [Milan Stock Exchange](/wiki/Borsa_Italiana "Borsa Italiana") (225 companies listed on the stock exchange) are both located in the city. Porta Nuova, the main [business district](/wiki/Business_district "Business district") of Milan and one of the most important in Europe, hosts the Italian headquarters of numerous global companies, such as [Accenture](/wiki/Accenture "Accenture"), [Axa](/wiki/Axa "Axa"), [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"), [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas "BNP Paribas"), [Celgene](/wiki/Celgene "Celgene"), [China Construction Bank](/wiki/China_Construction_Bank "China Construction Bank"), [Deutsche Bank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank_(Italy) "Deutsche Bank (Italy)"), [FM Global](/wiki/FM_Global "FM Global"), [Herbalife](/wiki/Herbalife "Herbalife"), [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)"), [Iliad](/wiki/Iliad_Italia "Iliad Italia"), [KPMG](/wiki/KPMG "KPMG"), [Maire Tecnimont](/wiki/Maire_Tecnimont "Maire Tecnimont"), [Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group](/wiki/Mitsubishi_UFJ_Financial_Group "Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group"), [Panasonic](/wiki/Panasonic "Panasonic"), [Pirelli](/wiki/Pirelli "Pirelli"), [Ubisoft](/wiki/Ubisoft_Milan "Ubisoft Milan"), [Shire](/wiki/Shire_(pharmaceutical_company) "Shire (pharmaceutical company)"), [Tata Consultancy Services](/wiki/Tata_Consultancy_Services "Tata Consultancy Services"), [Telecom Italia](/wiki/Telecom_Italia "Telecom Italia"), UniCredit and [UnipolSai](/wiki/UnipolSai "UnipolSai").

Other large multinational service companies, such as [Allianz](/wiki/Allianz "Allianz"), [Generali](/wiki/Assicurazioni_Generali "Assicurazioni Generali"), [Alleanza Assicurazioni](/wiki/Alleanza_Assicurazioni "Alleanza Assicurazioni") and [PwC](/wiki/PwC "PwC"), have their headquarters in the CityLife business district, a new 900-acre-wide (3.6 km2) development project designed by prominent modernist architects Zaha Hadid, Daniel Liebskind and Arata Isozaki.

The city is home to numerous media and advertising agencies, [national newspapers](/wiki/List_of_Italian_newspapers "List of Italian newspapers") and telecommunication companies, including both the public service broadcaster [RAI](/wiki/RAI "RAI") and private television companies like [Mediaset](/wiki/Mediaset "Mediaset") and [Sky Italia](/wiki/Sky_Italia "Sky Italia"). In addition, it hosts the headquarters of the largest Italian publishing companies, such as [Feltrinelli](/wiki/Feltrinelli_(publisher) "Feltrinelli (publisher)"), [Giunti Editore](/wiki/Giunti_Editore "Giunti Editore"), [Messaggerie Italiane](/w/index.php?title=Messaggerie_Italiane&action=edit&redlink=1 "Messaggerie Italiane (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Messaggerie_Italiane "it:Messaggerie Italiane")], [Mondadori](/wiki/Mondadori "Mondadori"), [RCS Media Group](/wiki/RCS_Media_Group "RCS Media Group") and [Rusconi Libri](/w/index.php?title=Rusconi_Libri&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rusconi Libri (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rusconi_Libri "it:Rusconi Libri")]. Milan has also seen a rapid increase in the presence of [IT](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology") companies, with both domestic and international companies such as [Altavista](/wiki/Altavista "Altavista"), Google, [Italtel](/wiki/Italtel "Italtel"), [Lycos](/wiki/Lycos "Lycos"), [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"),[[193]](#cite_note-195) [Virgilio](/wiki/Virgilio.it "Virgilio.it") and [Yahoo!](/wiki/Yahoo! "Yahoo!") establishing their Italian operations in the city.

The city is also a global hub for event management and trade fairs. [Fiera Milano](/wiki/Fiera_Milano "Fiera Milano") operates the most important trade fair organiser in Italy and the world's fourth-largest[[182]](#cite_note-Fiera-184) exhibition hall in Rho, were international exhibitions like [Milan Furniture Fair](/wiki/Milan_Furniture_Fair "Milan Furniture Fair"), [EICMA](/wiki/EICMA "EICMA"), [EMO](/wiki/EMO_(trade_show) "EMO (trade show)") take place on 400,000 square metres of exhibition areas with more than 4 million visitors in 2018.[[194]](#cite_note-196)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Main article: [Tourism in Milan](/wiki/Tourism_in_Milan "Tourism in Milan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/20170706_MilanoDuomo_9637_%2836747362195%29.jpg/960px-20170706_MilanoDuomo_9637_%2836747362195%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:20170706_MilanoDuomo_9637_(36747362195).jpg "The Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II, the Milan Cathedral, the Royal Palace of Milan and the Palazzo dell'Arengario on Piazza del Duomo")

The [Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II](/wiki/Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II "Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II"), the [Milan Cathedral](/wiki/Milan_Cathedral "Milan Cathedral"), the [Royal Palace of Milan](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Milan "Royal Palace of Milan") and the [Palazzo dell'Arengario](/wiki/Palazzo_dell%27Arengario "Palazzo dell'Arengario") on [Piazza del Duomo](/wiki/Piazza_del_Duomo,_Milan "Piazza del Duomo, Milan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Duomo_di_milano_keski.jpg/250px-Duomo_di_milano_keski.jpg)](/wiki/File:Duomo_di_milano_keski.jpg)

Interior of the [Milan Cathedral](/wiki/Milan_Cathedral "Milan Cathedral"), the city's most popular tourist destination.[[195]](#cite_note-slideshare-197)

[Tourism is an increasingly important part of the city's economy](/wiki/Tourism_in_Milan "Tourism in Milan"): with 8.81 million registered international arrivals in 2018 (up 9.92% on the previous year), Milan ranked as the world's 15th-most-visited city.[[196]](#cite_note-198) One source has 56% of international visitors to Milan are from Europe, 44% of the city's tourists are Italian, and 56% are from abroad.[[195]](#cite_note-slideshare-197) The most important European Union markets are the United Kingdom (16%), Germany (9%) and France (6%).[[195]](#cite_note-slideshare-197) Most of the visitors who come from the United States to the city go on business matters, while Chinese and Japanese tourists mainly take up the leisure segment.[[195]](#cite_note-slideshare-197) Milan is one of the international tourism destinations, appearing among the forty most visited cities in the world, ranking second in Italy after Rome, fifth in Europe and sixteenth in the world.[[23]](#cite_note-ilgiorno-25)[[24]](#cite_note-newsroom-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Via_Della_Spiga_%286602594603%29.jpg/250px-Via_Della_Spiga_%286602594603%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Via_Della_Spiga_(6602594603).jpg)

[Via della Spiga](/wiki/Via_della_Spiga "Via della Spiga"), one of the main shopping streets in Milan, is part of the luxurious *[Quadrilatero della Moda](/wiki/Quadrilatero_della_Moda "Quadrilatero della Moda")* (Fashion Quadrilateral) along with [Via Monte Napoleone](/wiki/Via_Monte_Napoleone "Via Monte Napoleone"), [Via Manzoni](/wiki/Via_Manzoni "Via Manzoni"), [Via Sant'Andrea](/wiki/Via_Sant%27Andrea "Via Sant'Andrea") and [Corso Venezia](/wiki/Corso_Venezia "Corso Venezia").

The city boasts several popular tourist attractions, such as the Milan Cathedral and Piazza del Duomo, the [Teatro alla Scala](/wiki/Teatro_alla_Scala "Teatro alla Scala"), the [San Siro Stadium](/wiki/San_Siro_Stadium "San Siro Stadium"), the Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II, the Castello Sforzesco, the Pinacoteca di Brera and the [Via Montenapoleone](/wiki/Via_Montenapoleone "Via Montenapoleone"). Most tourists visit sights[[197]](#cite_note-199) such as Milan Cathedral, the Castello Sforzesco and the Teatro alla Scala; however, other main sights such as the [Basilica di Sant'Ambrogio](/wiki/Basilica_di_Sant%27Ambrogio "Basilica di Sant'Ambrogio"), the Navigli and the Brera district are less visited and prove to be less popular.[[198]](#cite_note-slideshare.net-200) The city also has numerous hotels, including the ultra-luxurious [Town House Galleria](/wiki/Town_House_Galleria "Town House Galleria"), which is the world's first [seven-star](/wiki/Star_(classification) "Star (classification)") hotel according to [Société Générale de Surveillance](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_G%C3%A9n%C3%A9rale_de_Surveillance "Société Générale de Surveillance") (*five-star superior luxury* according to state law, however) and one of [The Leading Hotels of the World](/wiki/The_Leading_Hotels_of_the_World "The Leading Hotels of the World").[[199]](#cite_note-201)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of Milan](/wiki/Culture_of_Milan "Culture of Milan")

### Museums and art galleries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Museums and art galleries")]

Main article: [List of museums in Milan](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Milan "List of museums in Milan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Leonarda_da_vinci%2C_last_supper_01.jpg/250px-Leonarda_da_vinci%2C_last_supper_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonarda_da_vinci,_last_supper_01.jpg)

[Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci")'s *[The Last Supper](/wiki/The_Last_Supper_(Leonardo_da_Vinci) "The Last Supper (Leonardo da Vinci)")*, together with the church of [Santa Maria delle Grazie](/wiki/Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie_(Milan) "Santa Maria delle Grazie (Milan)"), is a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Milan_-_Pinacoth%C3%A8que_de_Brera_-_Cour_int%C3%A9rieure.jpg/250px-Milan_-_Pinacoth%C3%A8que_de_Brera_-_Cour_int%C3%A9rieure.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milan_-_Pinacoth%C3%A8que_de_Brera_-_Cour_int%C3%A9rieure.jpg)

[Pinacoteca di Brera](/wiki/Pinacoteca_di_Brera "Pinacoteca di Brera") is the city's largest art gallery.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Veduta_di_tre_quarti_del_Palazzo_Reale_di_Milano.jpg/250px-Veduta_di_tre_quarti_del_Palazzo_Reale_di_Milano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Veduta_di_tre_quarti_del_Palazzo_Reale_di_Milano.jpg)

The [Royal Palace of Milan](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Milan "Royal Palace of Milan") is a major venue for international art exhibitions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Arengario_Milano_Museo_900.jpg/250px-Arengario_Milano_Museo_900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arengario_Milano_Museo_900.jpg)

[Museo del Novecento](/wiki/Museo_del_Novecento "Museo del Novecento") covers 20th-century Italian art, including [Futurism](/wiki/Futurism "Futurism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Parco_Sempione_%28Milan%29%2C_Wikimania_2016%2C_MP_003.jpg/250px-Parco_Sempione_%28Milan%29%2C_Wikimania_2016%2C_MP_003.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parco_Sempione_(Milan),_Wikimania_2016,_MP_003.jpg)

[Triennale](/wiki/Triennale "Triennale") is a museum dedicated to architecture, design and fashion

Milan is home to many cultural institutions, museums and art galleries, that account for about a tenth of the national total of visitors and receipts.[[200]](#cite_note-202) The [Pinacoteca di Brera](/wiki/Pinacoteca_di_Brera "Pinacoteca di Brera") is one of Milan's most important art galleries. It contains one of the foremost collections of Italian painting, including masterpieces such as the *[Brera Madonna](/wiki/Brera_Madonna "Brera Madonna")* by [Piero della Francesca](/wiki/Piero_della_Francesca "Piero della Francesca"). The [Castello Sforzesco](/wiki/Castello_Sforzesco "Castello Sforzesco") hosts numerous art collections and exhibitions, especially statues, ancient arms and furnitures, as well as the [Pinacoteca del Castello Sforzesco](/wiki/Sforza_Castle_Pinacoteca "Sforza Castle Pinacoteca"), with an art collection including [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo")'s last sculpture, the *[Rondanini Pietà](/wiki/Rondanini_Piet%C3%A0 "Rondanini Pietà")*, [Andrea Mantegna](/wiki/Andrea_Mantegna "Andrea Mantegna")'s *[Trivulzio Madonna](/wiki/Trivulzio_Madonna "Trivulzio Madonna")* and Leonardo da Vinci's *[Codex Trivulzianus](/wiki/Codex_Trivulzianus "Codex Trivulzianus")* manuscript. The Castello complex also includes [The Museum of Ancient Art](/wiki/Museo_d%27Arte_Antica "Museo d'Arte Antica"), The Furniture Museum, The [Museum of Musical Instruments](/wiki/Museum_of_Musical_Instruments_(Milan) "Museum of Musical Instruments (Milan)") and the [Applied Arts Collection](/wiki/Applied_Arts_Collection_(Milan) "Applied Arts Collection (Milan)"), [The Egyptian and Prehistoric sections](/wiki/Egyptian_Museum_(Milan) "Egyptian Museum (Milan)") of the [Archaeological Museum](/wiki/Archaeological_Museum_(Milan) "Archaeological Museum (Milan)") and the Achille Bertarelli Print Collection (Civica Raccolta delle Stampe Bertarelli).

Milan's figurative art flourished in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), and with the Visconti family being major patrons of the arts, the city became an important centre of [Gothic art](/wiki/Gothic_art "Gothic art") and architecture (Milan Cathedral being the city's most formidable work of Gothic architecture). Leonardo worked in Milan from 1482 until 1499. He was commissioned to paint the *[Virgin of the Rocks](/wiki/Virgin_of_the_Rocks "Virgin of the Rocks")* for the Confraternity of the Immaculate Conception and *[The Last Supper](/wiki/The_Last_Supper_(Leonardo) "The Last Supper (Leonardo)")* for the monastery of [Santa Maria delle Grazie](/wiki/Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie_(Milan) "Santa Maria delle Grazie (Milan)").[[201]](#cite_note-Kemp-203)

The city was [affected by the Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_in_Milan "Baroque in Milan") in the 17th and 18th centuries, and hosted numerous formidable artists, architects and painters of that period, such as [Caravaggio](/wiki/Caravaggio "Caravaggio") and [Francesco Hayez](/wiki/Francesco_Hayez "Francesco Hayez"), which several important works are hosted in Brera Academy. The [Museum of Risorgimento](/wiki/Museum_of_the_Risorgimento_(Milan) "Museum of the Risorgimento (Milan)") is specialised on the history of [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") Its collections include iconic paintings like [Baldassare Verazzi](/wiki/Baldassare_Verazzi "Baldassare Verazzi")'s *Episode from the Five Days* and Francesco Hayez's 1840 *[Portrait](/wiki/File:Francesco_Hayez_047.jpg "File:Francesco Hayez 047.jpg") of Emperor [Ferdinand I of Austria](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_Austria "Ferdinand I of Austria")*. The [Triennale](/wiki/Triennale "Triennale") is a design museum and events venue located in Palazzo dell'Arte, in [Sempione Park](/wiki/Parco_Sempione "Parco Sempione"). It hosts exhibitions and events highlighting contemporary Italian design, urban planning, architecture, music and media arts, emphasising the relationship between art and industry.

Milan in the 20th century was the epicentre of the [futurist](/wiki/Futurism "Futurism") artistic movement. [Filippo Marinetti](/wiki/Filippo_Tommaso_Marinetti "Filippo Tommaso Marinetti"), the founder of Italian Futurism wrote in his 1909 "*[Manifesto of Futurism](/wiki/Manifesto_of_Futurism "Manifesto of Futurism")*" (in Italian, *Manifesto Futuristico*), that Milan was "*grande...tradizionale e futurista*" ("*grand...traditional and futuristic*", in English). [Umberto Boccioni](/wiki/Umberto_Boccioni "Umberto Boccioni") was also an important Futurism artist who worked in the city. Today, Milan remains a major international hub of modern and contemporary art, with numerous modern art galleries. The [Modern Art Gallery](/wiki/Modern_Art_Gallery_(Milan) "Modern Art Gallery (Milan)"), situated in the Royal Villa, hosts collections of Italian and European painting from the 18th to the early 20th centuries.[[202]](#cite_note-204)[[203]](#cite_note-205)[[204]](#cite_note-206) The [Museo del Novecento](/wiki/The_Museum_of_Twentieth_Century_(Museo_del_Novecento) "The Museum of Twentieth Century (Museo del Novecento)"), situated in the [Palazzo dell'Arengario](/wiki/Palazzo_dell%27Arengario "Palazzo dell'Arengario"), is one of the most important art galleries in Italy about 20th-century art; of particular relevance are the sections dedicated to Futurism, [Spatialism](/wiki/Spatialism "Spatialism") and [Arte povera](/wiki/Arte_povera "Arte povera"). In the early 1990s architect [David Chipperfield](/wiki/David_Chipperfield "David Chipperfield") was invited to convert the premises of the former Ansaldo Factory into a Museum. Museo delle Culture (MUDEC) opened in April 2015.[[205]](#cite_note-207) The [Gallerie di Piazza Scala](/wiki/Gallerie_di_Piazza_Scala "Gallerie di Piazza Scala"), a modern and contemporary museum located in Piazza della Scala in the Palazzo Brentani and the Palazzo Anguissola, hosts 195 artworks from the collections of [Fondazione Cariplo](/wiki/Fondazione_Cariplo "Fondazione Cariplo") with a strong representation of nineteenth-century Lombard painters and sculptors, including [Antonio Canova](/wiki/Antonio_Canova "Antonio Canova") and Umberto Boccioni. A new section was opened in the Palazzo della Banca Commerciale Italiana in 2012. Other private ventures dedicated to contemporary art include the exhibiting spaces of the [Prada Foundation](/wiki/Prada_Foundation "Prada Foundation") and [HangarBicocca](/wiki/HangarBicocca "HangarBicocca"). The [Nicola Trussardi Foundation](/wiki/Nicola_Trussardi_Foundation "Nicola Trussardi Foundation") is renewed for organising temporary exhibition in venues around the city. Milan is also home to many public art projects, with a variety of works that range from sculptures to murals to pieces by internationally renowned artists, including [Arman](/wiki/Arman "Arman"), [Kengiro Azuma](/wiki/Kengiro_Azuma "Kengiro Azuma"), [Francesco Barzaghi](/wiki/Francesco_Barzaghi "Francesco Barzaghi"), [Alberto Burri](/wiki/Alberto_Burri "Alberto Burri"), [Pietro Cascella](/wiki/Pietro_Cascella "Pietro Cascella"), [Maurizio Cattelan](/wiki/Maurizio_Cattelan "Maurizio Cattelan"), Leonardo da Vinci, [Giorgio de Chirico](/wiki/Giorgio_de_Chirico "Giorgio de Chirico"), [Kris Ruhs](/wiki/Kris_Ruhs "Kris Ruhs"), [Emilio Isgrò](/wiki/Emilio_Isgr%C3%B2 "Emilio Isgrò"), [Fausto Melotti](/wiki/Fausto_Melotti "Fausto Melotti"), [Joan Miró](/wiki/Joan_Mir%C3%B3 "Joan Miró"), Carlo Mo, [Claes Oldenburg](/wiki/Claes_Oldenburg "Claes Oldenburg"), [Igor Mitoraj](/wiki/Igor_Mitoraj "Igor Mitoraj"), Gianfranco Pardi, [Michelangelo Pistoletto](/wiki/Michelangelo_Pistoletto "Michelangelo Pistoletto"), [Arnaldo Pomodoro](/wiki/Arnaldo_Pomodoro "Arnaldo Pomodoro"), Carlo Ramous, [Aldo Rossi](/wiki/Aldo_Rossi "Aldo Rossi"), [Aligi Sassu](/wiki/Aligi_Sassu "Aligi Sassu"), [Giuseppe Spagnulo](/wiki/Giuseppe_Spagnulo "Giuseppe Spagnulo") and [Domenico Trentacoste](/wiki/Domenico_Trentacoste "Domenico Trentacoste").

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Music")]

See also: [Music of Milan](/wiki/Music_of_Milan "Music of Milan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Milano_-_Teatro_alla_Scala_3924.jpg/330px-Milano_-_Teatro_alla_Scala_3924.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milano_-_Teatro_alla_Scala_3924.jpg)

Inaugurated in 1778, [La Scala](/wiki/La_Scala "La Scala") is among the world's most famous opera houses.

Milan is considered an international capital of the performing arts. The city hosts [La Scala](/wiki/La_Scala "La Scala") opera house, widely considered one of the world's most prestigious,[[206]](#cite_note-208) with a 200-year history of [premieres](/wiki/Premier_Exhibitions "Premier Exhibitions") of major operas, such as *[Nabucco](/wiki/Nabucco "Nabucco")* by [Giuseppe Verdi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Verdi "Giuseppe Verdi") in 1842, *[La Gioconda](/wiki/La_Gioconda_(opera) "La Gioconda (opera)")* by [Amilcare Ponchielli](/wiki/Amilcare_Ponchielli "Amilcare Ponchielli"), *[Madama Butterfly](/wiki/Madama_Butterfly "Madama Butterfly")* by [Giacomo Puccini](/wiki/Giacomo_Puccini "Giacomo Puccini") in 1904, *[Turandot](/wiki/Turandot "Turandot")* by Puccini in 1926, and more recently *[Teneke](/wiki/Teneke_(opera) "Teneke (opera)")*, by [Fabio Vacchi](/wiki/Fabio_Vacchi "Fabio Vacchi") in 2007. Other relevant theatres in Milan include [Teatro degli Arcimboldi](/wiki/Teatro_degli_Arcimboldi "Teatro degli Arcimboldi"), [Teatro Dal Verme](/wiki/Teatro_Dal_Verme "Teatro Dal Verme"), [Teatro Lirico](/wiki/Teatro_Lirico_(Milan) "Teatro Lirico (Milan)"). The city is also the seat of a renowned [symphony orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra_Sinfonica_di_Milano_Giuseppe_Verdi "Orchestra Sinfonica di Milano Giuseppe Verdi") and [musical conservatory](/wiki/Milan_Conservatory "Milan Conservatory"), and has been, throughout history, a major centre for musical composition: numerous famous composers and musicians such as [Gioseppe Caimo](/wiki/Gioseppe_Caimo "Gioseppe Caimo"), [Simon Boyleau](/wiki/Simon_Boyleau "Simon Boyleau"), [Hoste da Reggio](/wiki/Hoste_da_Reggio "Hoste da Reggio"), Giuseppe Verdi, [Giulio Gatti-Casazza](/wiki/Giulio_Gatti-Casazza "Giulio Gatti-Casazza"), [Paolo Cherici](/wiki/Paolo_Cherici "Paolo Cherici") and [Alice Edun](/wiki/Alice_Edun "Alice Edun") lived and worked in Milan. The city is also the birthplace of many modern ensembles and bands, including [I Camaleonti](/wiki/I_Camaleonti "I Camaleonti"), [Camerata Mediolanense](/wiki/Camerata_Mediolanense "Camerata Mediolanense"), [Gli Spioni](/wiki/Gli_Spioni "Gli Spioni"), [Dynamis Ensemble](/wiki/Dynamis_Ensemble "Dynamis Ensemble"), [Elio e le Storie Tese](/wiki/Elio_e_le_Storie_Tese "Elio e le Storie Tese"), [Krisma](/wiki/Krisma "Krisma"), [Premiata Forneria Marconi](/wiki/Premiata_Forneria_Marconi "Premiata Forneria Marconi"), [Quartetto Cetra](/wiki/Quartetto_Cetra "Quartetto Cetra"), [Stormy Six](/wiki/Stormy_Six "Stormy Six"), [Le Vibrazioni](/wiki/Le_Vibrazioni "Le Vibrazioni") and [Lacuna Coil](/wiki/Lacuna_Coil "Lacuna Coil").

### Fashion and design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Fashion and design")]

Main article: [Fashion in Milan](/wiki/Fashion_in_Milan "Fashion in Milan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/1952_Alfa_Romeo_1900_C52_Disco_Volante_Spider_ARM.jpg/250px-1952_Alfa_Romeo_1900_C52_Disco_Volante_Spider_ARM.jpg)](/wiki/File:1952_Alfa_Romeo_1900_C52_Disco_Volante_Spider_ARM.jpg)

A 1952 [Alfa Romeo 1900 C52 Disco Volante](/wiki/Alfa_Romeo_Disco_Volante "Alfa Romeo Disco Volante") at the [Museum](/wiki/Museo_Alfa_Romeo "Museo Alfa Romeo") of [Alfa Romeo](/wiki/Alfa_Romeo "Alfa Romeo"), established in Milan in 1910. The company's logo features the [flag of Milan](/wiki/Flag_of_Milan "Flag of Milan") and the [Biscione](/wiki/Biscione "Biscione"), a symbol of the city, originally on the coat-of-arms of the [Visconti](/wiki/Visconti_of_Milan "Visconti of Milan") and [Sforza](/wiki/House_of_Sforza "House of Sforza") dynasties.

Milan is widely regarded as a global capital in industrial design, fashion and architecture.[[207]](#cite_note-209) In the 1950s and 60s, as the main industrial centre of Italy and one of Europe's most dynamic cities, Milan became a world capital of design and architecture. There was such a revolutionary change that Milan's fashion exports accounted for US$726 million in 1952, and by 1955 that number grew to US$72.5 billion.[[208]](#cite_note-210)

Modern skyscrapers, such as the [Pirelli Tower](/wiki/Pirelli_Tower "Pirelli Tower") and the [Torre Velasca](/wiki/Torre_Velasca "Torre Velasca") were built, and artists such as [Bruno Munari](/wiki/Bruno_Munari "Bruno Munari"), [Lucio Fontana](/wiki/Lucio_Fontana "Lucio Fontana"), [Enrico Castellani](/wiki/Enrico_Castellani "Enrico Castellani") and [Piero Manzoni](/wiki/Piero_Manzoni "Piero Manzoni") gathered in the city.[[209]](#cite_note-211) Today, Milan is still particularly well known for its high-quality furniture and interior design industry. The city is home to [Fiera Milano](/wiki/Fiera_Milano "Fiera Milano"), Europe's largest permanent trade exhibition, and Salone Internazionale del Mobile, one of the most prestigious international furniture and design fairs.[[210]](#cite_note-212)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II_2382.jpg/250px-Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II_2382.jpg)](/wiki/File:Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II_2382.jpg)

[Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II](/wiki/Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II "Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II") is Italy's oldest active shopping gallery and a major landmark of Milan.

Milan is also regarded as one of the fashion capitals of the world, along with [New York City](/wiki/New_York_Fashion_Week "New York Fashion Week"), [Paris](/wiki/Paris_Fashion_Week "Paris Fashion Week") and [London](/wiki/London_Fashion_Week "London Fashion Week").[[211]](#cite_note-213) Milan is synonymous with the Italian prêt-à-porter industry,[[212]](#cite_note-214) as many of the most famous [Italian fashion](/wiki/Italian_fashion "Italian fashion") brands, such as [Valentino](/wiki/Valentino_SpA "Valentino SpA"), [Versace](/wiki/Versace "Versace"), [Prada](/wiki/Prada "Prada"), [Armani](/wiki/Armani "Armani") and [Dolce & Gabbana](/wiki/Dolce_%26_Gabbana "Dolce & Gabbana"), are headquartered in the city. Numerous international fashion labels also operate shops in Milan. Furthermore, the city hosts the [Milan Fashion Week](/wiki/Milan_Fashion_Week "Milan Fashion Week") twice a year, one of the most important events in the international fashion system.[[213]](#cite_note-215) Milan's main upscale fashion district, *[quadrilatero della moda](/wiki/Quadrilatero_della_moda "Quadrilatero della moda")*, is home to the city's most prestigious shopping streets ([Via Monte Napoleone](/wiki/Via_Monte_Napoleone "Via Monte Napoleone"), [Via della Spiga](/wiki/Via_della_Spiga "Via della Spiga"), [Via Sant'Andrea](/wiki/Via_Sant%27Andrea "Via Sant'Andrea"), [Via Manzoni](/wiki/Via_Manzoni "Via Manzoni") and [Corso Venezia](/wiki/Corso_Venezia "Corso Venezia")), in addition to [Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II](/wiki/Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II "Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II"), one of the world's oldest shopping malls.[[214]](#cite_note-216) The term *[sciura](/wiki/Sciura "Sciura")* encapsulates the look and culture of fashionable, elderly Milanese women.

### Languages and literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Languages and literature")]

Main article: [Milanese literature](/wiki/Milanese_literature "Milanese literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Francesco_Hayez_-_Ritratto_di_Alessandro_Manzoni.jpg/250px-Francesco_Hayez_-_Ritratto_di_Alessandro_Manzoni.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francesco_Hayez_-_Ritratto_di_Alessandro_Manzoni.jpg)

[Alessandro Manzoni](/wiki/Alessandro_Manzoni "Alessandro Manzoni") is famous for the novel *[The Betrothed](/wiki/The_Betrothed "The Betrothed")* (1827), generally ranked among the masterpieces of [world literature](/wiki/World_literature "World literature").[[215]](#cite_note-britannica-217) This novel is a fundamental milestone in the development of the modern, unified Italian language.[[216]](#cite_note-218)

In the late 18th century and throughout the 19th, Milan was an important centre for intellectual discussion and literary creativity. The [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") found here a fertile ground. [Cesare, Marquis of Beccaria](/wiki/Cesare_Beccaria "Cesare Beccaria"), with his famous *[Dei delitti e delle pene](/wiki/Dei_delitti_e_delle_pene "Dei delitti e delle pene")*, and Count [Pietro Verri](/wiki/Pietro_Verri "Pietro Verri"), with the periodical *Il Caffè* were able to exert a considerable influence over the new [middle-class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class") culture.

In the first years of the 19th century, the ideals of the [Romantic movement](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") made their impact on the cultural life of the city and its major writers debated the primacy of Classical versus [Romantic poetry](/wiki/Romantic_poetry "Romantic poetry"). Additionally, [Giuseppe Parini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Parini "Giuseppe Parini") and [Ugo Foscolo](/wiki/Ugo_Foscolo "Ugo Foscolo") published their most important works, and were admired by younger poets as masters of ethics, as well as of literary craftsmanship.

In the third decade of the 19th century, [Alessandro Manzoni](/wiki/Alessandro_Manzoni "Alessandro Manzoni") wrote his novel *[The Betrothed](/wiki/The_Betrothed "The Betrothed")*, considered the manifesto of Italian Romanticism, which found in Milan its centre; in the same period [Carlo Porta](/wiki/Carlo_Porta "Carlo Porta"), reputed the most renowned local vernacular poet, wrote his poems in [Lombard Language](/wiki/Lombard_Language "Lombard Language"). The periodical *[Il Conciliatore](/wiki/Il_Conciliatore "Il Conciliatore")* published articles by [Silvio Pellico](/wiki/Silvio_Pellico "Silvio Pellico"), [Giovanni Berchet](/wiki/Giovanni_Berchet "Giovanni Berchet"), [Ludovico di Breme](/wiki/Ludovico_di_Breme "Ludovico di Breme"), who were both Romantic in poetry and patriotic in politics.

After the Unification of Italy in 1861, Milan retained a sort of central position in cultural debates. New ideas and movements from other countries of Europe were accepted and discussed: thus [Realism](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)") and [Naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(literature) "Naturalism (literature)") gave birth to prewar Italian movement of *[Verismo](/wiki/Verismo "Verismo")* in Southern Italy, its greatest *Verista* novelist [Giovanni Verga](/wiki/Giovanni_Verga "Giovanni Verga") formed in Sicily who wrote his most important books in Milan.

In addition to Italian, approximately 2 million people in Northern Italy can speak the [Milanese dialect](/wiki/Milanese_dialect "Milanese dialect") or other [Western Lombard](/wiki/Western_dialects_of_Lombard_language "Western dialects of Lombard language") varieties.[[217]](#cite_note-219)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Media")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/IMG_4263_-_Milano_-_Sede_del_Corriere_della_Sera_in_via_Solferino_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto_20-jan_2007.jpg/250px-IMG_4263_-_Milano_-_Sede_del_Corriere_della_Sera_in_via_Solferino_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto_20-jan_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:IMG_4263_-_Milano_-_Sede_del_Corriere_della_Sera_in_via_Solferino_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto_20-jan_2007.jpg)

The historic headquarters of *[Corriere della Sera](/wiki/Corriere_della_Sera "Corriere della Sera")* in via Solferino

Milan is an important national and international media center. [RCS](/wiki/RCS_MediaGroup "RCS MediaGroup"), a major international multimedia publishing group, is the publisher of both *[Corriere della Sera](/wiki/Corriere_della_Sera "Corriere della Sera")* (established in 1876, it is one of the oldest Italian newspapers) and *[La Gazzetta dello Sport](/wiki/La_Gazzetta_dello_Sport "La Gazzetta dello Sport")* (a daily dedicated to sports, and the most widely-read in Italy). Other local dailies are the general broadsheets *[Il Giorno](/wiki/Il_Giorno_(newspaper) "Il Giorno (newspaper)")*, *[Il Giornale](/wiki/Il_Giornale "Il Giornale")*, the Catholic newspaper *[Avvenire](/wiki/Avvenire "Avvenire")*, and *[Il Sole 24 Ore](/wiki/Il_Sole_24_Ore "Il Sole 24 Ore")*, a daily business newspaper owned by [Confindustria](/wiki/Confindustria "Confindustria") (the Italian employers' federation). Free daily newspapers include *[Leggo](/wiki/Leggo "Leggo")* and *[Metro](/wiki/Metro_(Italian_newspaper) "Metro (Italian newspaper)")*. Milan is also home to many architecture, art and fashion periodicals, including *[Abitare](/wiki/Abitare "Abitare")*, *[Casabella](/wiki/Casabella "Casabella")*, *[Domus](/wiki/Domus_(magazine) "Domus (magazine)")*, *[Flash Art](/wiki/Flash_Art "Flash Art")*, *[Gioia](/wiki/Gioia_(magazine) "Gioia (magazine)")*, *[Grazia](/wiki/Grazia "Grazia")* and *[Vogue Italia](/wiki/Vogue_Italia "Vogue Italia")*. *[Panorama](/wiki/Panorama_(magazine) "Panorama (magazine)")* and *[Oggi](/wiki/Oggi_(magazine) "Oggi (magazine)")*, two of Italy's most important weekly news magazines, are also published in Milan. Several major television networks have their national headquarters in Milan and its metropolitan area, including [Mediaset](/wiki/Mediaset "Mediaset") (the largest commercial broadcaster in the country), [Sky Italia](/wiki/Sky_Italia "Sky Italia"), the Italian division of [Paramount Skydance](/wiki/Paramount_Skydance "Paramount Skydance"),[[218]](#cite_note-220) [Telelombardia](/wiki/Telelombardia "Telelombardia"). National radio stations based in Milan include [Radio DeeJay](/wiki/Radio_DeeJay "Radio DeeJay"), [Radio 105 Network](/wiki/Radio_105_Network "Radio 105 Network"), [R101 (Italy)](/wiki/R101_(Italy) "R101 (Italy)"), [Radio Popolare](/wiki/Radio_Popolare "Radio Popolare"), [RTL 102.5](/wiki/RTL_102.5 "RTL 102.5"), [Radio Capital](/wiki/Radio_Capital "Radio Capital") and [Virgin Radio Italia](/wiki/Virgin_Radio_Italia "Virgin Radio Italia").

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [Lombard cuisine](/wiki/Lombard_cuisine "Lombard cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Redaktionsvortreffen_EuT_2_ossobuco_16.04.2011_22-59-12.2011_22-59-12.jpg/250px-Redaktionsvortreffen_EuT_2_ossobuco_16.04.2011_22-59-12.2011_22-59-12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Redaktionsvortreffen_EuT_2_ossobuco_16.04.2011_22-59-12.2011_22-59-12.jpg)

[Ossobuco](/wiki/Ossobuco "Ossobuco") served with *[risotto alla milanese](/wiki/Risotto_alla_milanese "Risotto alla milanese")*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Panettone_vero.jpg/250px-Panettone_vero.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panettone_vero.jpg)

[Panettone](/wiki/Panettone "Panettone")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Antica_trattoria_Bagutto.jpg/250px-Antica_trattoria_Bagutto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Antica_trattoria_Bagutto.jpg)

The [Antica trattoria Bagutto](/wiki/Antica_trattoria_Bagutto "Antica trattoria Bagutto") in Milan, the oldest restaurant in Italy and the second oldest in Europe.[[219]](#cite_note-localistorici-221)

Like most cities in Italy, Milan has developed its own local culinary tradition, which, as it is typical for North Italian cuisines, uses more frequently rice than pasta, butter than [vegetable oil](/wiki/Vegetable_oil "Vegetable oil") and features almost no tomato or [fish](/wiki/Fish_as_food "Fish as food"). Milanese traditional dishes include *[cotoletta alla milanese](/wiki/Cotoletta "Cotoletta")*, a breaded veal (pork and turkey can be used) cutlet pan-fried in butter (similar to Viennese [Wiener Schnitzel](/wiki/Wiener_Schnitzel "Wiener Schnitzel")). Other typical dishes are *[cassoeula](/wiki/Cassoeula "Cassoeula")* (stewed pork rib chops and sausage with [Savoy cabbage](/wiki/Cabbage "Cabbage")), *[ossobuco](/wiki/Ossobuco "Ossobuco")* (braised [veal](/wiki/Veal "Veal") shank served with a condiment called *[gremolata](/wiki/Gremolata "Gremolata")*), *[risotto alla milanese](/wiki/Risotto "Risotto")* (with [saffron](/wiki/Saffron "Saffron") and beef marrow), *busecca* (stewed [tripe](/wiki/Tripe "Tripe") with beans), *[mondeghili](/wiki/Mondeghili "Mondeghili")* ([meatballs](/wiki/Meatballs "Meatballs") made with leftover meat fried in butter) and *brasato* (stewed beef or pork with wine and potatoes).

Season-related pastries include *chiacchiere* (flat fritters dusted with sugar) and *tortelli* (fried spherical cookies) for [Carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival"), *colomba* (glazed cake shaped as a dove) for Easter, *pane dei morti* ("bread of the (Day of the) Dead", cookies flavoured with [cinnamon](/wiki/Cinnamon "Cinnamon")) for [All Souls' Day](/wiki/All_Souls%27_Day "All Souls' Day") and [panettone](/wiki/Panettone "Panettone") for Christmas. The *salame Milano*, a [salami](/wiki/Salami "Salami") with a very fine grain, is widespread throughout Italy. Renowned Milanese cheeses are [gorgonzola](/wiki/Gorgonzola "Gorgonzola") (from the [namesake](/wiki/Gorgonzola,_Milan "Gorgonzola, Milan") village nearby), [mascarpone](/wiki/Mascarpone "Mascarpone"), used in pastry-making, [taleggio](/wiki/Taleggio_cheese "Taleggio cheese") and [quartirolo](/wiki/Quartirolo_lombardo "Quartirolo lombardo").

The *[comune](/wiki/Comune "Comune")* of [San Colombano al Lambro](/wiki/San_Colombano_al_Lambro "San Colombano al Lambro"), located about 40 kilometres (25 mi) south-east of Milan, is home to the *[Denominazione di origine controllata](/wiki/Denominazione_di_origine_controllata "Denominazione di origine controllata")* (DOC) wine which includes 100 hectares (250 acres) producing a single red wine. The finished wine must attain a minimum [alcohol level](/wiki/Alcohol_level "Alcohol level") of 11% to be [labelled](/wiki/Labelled_(wine) "Labelled (wine)") with the San Colombano DOC designation.[[220]](#cite_note-Saunders-222)

Milan is well known for its world-class restaurants and cafés, characterised by innovative cuisine and design.[[221]](#cite_note-223) As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit), Milan has 157 Michelin-selected places, including three 1-Michelin-starred restaurants;[[222]](#cite_note-224) these include [Cracco](/wiki/Cracco_in_Galleria "Cracco in Galleria"), Sadler and il Luogo di Aimo e Nadia.[[223]](#cite_note-225) Many historical restaurants and bars are found in the historic centre, the Brera and Navigli districts. Milan is home to the oldest restaurant in Italy and the second in Europe, the [Antica trattoria Bagutto](/wiki/Antica_trattoria_Bagutto "Antica trattoria Bagutto"), which has existed since at least 1284.[[219]](#cite_note-localistorici-221) One of the city's oldest surviving cafés, [Caffè Cova](/wiki/Caff%C3%A8_Cova "Caffè Cova"), was established in 1817.[[224]](#cite_note-226) In total, Milan has 15 cafés, bars and restaurants registered among the Historical Places of Italy, continuously operating for at least 70 years.[[225]](#cite_note-227)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/af/Stadio_Meazza_2021.jpg/250px-Stadio_Meazza_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stadio_Meazza_2021.jpg)

[San Siro Stadium](/wiki/San_Siro "San Siro"), home of [AC Milan](/wiki/AC_Milan "AC Milan") and [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan"), has a capacity of 80,000. It is Italy's biggest stadium.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Forum_Assago_Parquet_2.jpg/250px-Forum_Assago_Parquet_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Forum_Assago_Parquet_2.jpg)

[Mediolanum Forum](/wiki/Mediolanum_Forum "Mediolanum Forum"), home of [Olimpia Milano](/wiki/Olimpia_Milano "Olimpia Milano")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Autodromo_Nazionale_Monza%2C_April_22%2C_2018_SkySat_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Autodromo_Nazionale_Monza%2C_April_22%2C_2018_SkySat_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Autodromo_Nazionale_Monza,_April_22,_2018_SkySat_(cropped).jpg)

Satellite view of the [Monza Circuit](/wiki/Monza_Circuit "Monza Circuit")

Milan hosted matches at the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") in [1934](/wiki/1934_FIFA_World_Cup "1934 FIFA World Cup") and [1990](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup"), the [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") in [1980](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1980 "UEFA Euro 1980"), and more recently held the [2003 World Rowing Championships](/wiki/2003_World_Rowing_Championships "2003 World Rowing Championships"), the [2009 World Boxing Championships](/wiki/2009_AIBA_World_Boxing_Championships "2009 AIBA World Boxing Championships"), and some games of the [Men's Volleyball World Championship](/wiki/FIVB_Volleyball_Men%27s_World_Championship "FIVB Volleyball Men's World Championship") in [2010](/wiki/2010_FIVB_Volleyball_Men%27s_World_Championship "2010 FIVB Volleyball Men's World Championship"), as well as the final games of the [Women's Volleyball World Championship](/wiki/FIVB_Volleyball_Women%27s_World_Championship "FIVB Volleyball Women's World Championship") in [2014](/wiki/2014_FIVB_Volleyball_Women%27s_World_Championship "2014 FIVB Volleyball Women's World Championship"). In 2018, Milan hosted the [World Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships"). Milan also hosted the [2026 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2026_Winter_Olympics "2026 Winter Olympics") as well as the [2026 Winter Paralympics](/wiki/2026_Winter_Paralympics "2026 Winter Paralympics") jointly with [Cortina d'Ampezzo](/wiki/Cortina_d%27Ampezzo "Cortina d'Ampezzo").

Milan, along with [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), is one of only two cities in Europe that is home to two [European Cup/Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") winning teams: [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") football clubs [AC Milan](/wiki/AC_Milan "AC Milan") and [Inter](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan"). They are two of the most successful clubs in the world of football in terms of international trophies. Both teams have also won the [FIFA Club World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Club_World_Cup "FIFA Club World Cup") (formerly the [Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/Intercontinental_Cup_(1960%E2%80%932004) "Intercontinental Cup (1960–2004)")). With a combined ten Champions League titles, Milan is only second to Madrid as the city with the most European Cups. Both teams play at the [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") 5-star-rated Giuseppe Meazza Stadium, more commonly known as the [San Siro](/wiki/San_Siro "San Siro"), that is one of the biggest stadiums in Europe, with a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of over 80,000.[[226]](#cite_note-size-228) The Meazza Stadium has hosted four [European Cup/Champions League finals](/wiki/List_of_European_Cup_and_UEFA_Champions_League_finals "List of European Cup and UEFA Champions League finals"), most recently in [2016](/wiki/2016_UEFA_Champions_League_final "2016 UEFA Champions League final"), when [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_CF "Real Madrid CF") defeated [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid") 5–3 in a [penalty shoot-out](/wiki/Penalty_shoot-out_(association_football) "Penalty shoot-out (association football)"). A third team, [Brera Calcio](/wiki/Brera_Calcio "Brera Calcio"), plays in [Prima Categoria](/wiki/Prima_Categoria "Prima Categoria"), the seventh tier of Italian football.[[227]](#cite_note-229) Another team, [Milano City FC](/wiki/Milano_City_FC "Milano City FC") (a successor of Bustese Calcio),[[228]](#cite_note-230) plays in [Serie D](/wiki/Serie_D "Serie D"), the fourth level.

Milan was one of the host cities of the [EuroBasket 2022](/wiki/EuroBasket_2022 "EuroBasket 2022"). There are currently four professional [Lega Basket](/wiki/Lega_Basket "Lega Basket") clubs in Milan: [Olimpia Milano](/wiki/Olimpia_Milano "Olimpia Milano"), Pallacanestro Milano 1958, Società Canottieri Milano and A.S.S.I. Milano. Olimpia is the most decorated basketball club in Italy, having won 31 [Italian League](/wiki/Lega_Basket_Serie_A "Lega Basket Serie A") championships, eight [Italian National Cups](/wiki/Italian_Basketball_Cup "Italian Basketball Cup"), five [Italian Super Cups](/wiki/Italian_Basketball_Supercup "Italian Basketball Supercup"), three [European Champions Cups](/wiki/EuroLeague "EuroLeague"), one [FIBA Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/FIBA_Intercontinental_Cup "FIBA Intercontinental Cup"), three [FIBA Saporta Cups](/wiki/FIBA_Saporta_Cup "FIBA Saporta Cup"), two [FIBA Korać Cups](/wiki/FIBA_Kora%C4%87_Cup "FIBA Korać Cup") and many junior titles. The team play at the [Mediolanum Forum](/wiki/Mediolanum_Forum "Mediolanum Forum"), with a capacity of 12,700, where it has been hosted the final of the [2013–14 Euroleague](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Euroleague "2013–14 Euroleague"). In some cases the team also plays at the [PalaDesio](/wiki/PalaDesio "PalaDesio"), with a capacity of 6,700.

Milan is also home to Italy's oldest American football team: [Rhinos Milano](/wiki/Rhinos_Milano "Rhinos Milano"), who have won five Italian Super Bowls. The team plays at the [Velodromo Vigorelli](/wiki/Velodromo_Vigorelli "Velodromo Vigorelli"), with a capacity of 8,000. Another [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") team that use the same venue is the [Seamen Milano](/wiki/Milano_Seamen "Milano Seamen"), who joined the professional [European League of Football](/wiki/European_League_of_Football "European League of Football") in 2023. Milan also has two cricket teams: Milano Fiori, currently competing in the second division, and Kingsgrove Milan, who won the Serie A championship in 2014. [Amatori Rugby Milano](/wiki/Amatori_Rugby_Milano "Amatori Rugby Milano"), the most decorated rugby team in Italy, was founded in Milan in 1927. The [Monza Circuit](/wiki/Monza_Circuit "Monza Circuit"), located near Milan, hosts the Formula One [Italian Grand Prix](/wiki/Italian_Grand_Prix "Italian Grand Prix").[[229]](#cite_note-231) The circuit is located inside the [Royal Villa of Monza](/wiki/Royal_Villa_of_Monza "Royal Villa of Monza") park. It is one of the world's oldest [car racing](/wiki/Auto_racing "Auto racing") circuits. The capacity for the [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") races is currently over 113,000. It has hosted a Formula One race nearly every year since the first year of competition, with the exception of 1980.

In [road cycling](/wiki/Road_bicycle_racing "Road bicycle racing"), Milan hosts the start of the annual [Milan–San Remo](/wiki/Milan%E2%80%93San_Remo "Milan–San Remo") [classic](/wiki/Classic_cycle_races "Classic cycle races") one-day race and the annual [Milano–Torino](/wiki/Milano%E2%80%93Torino "Milano–Torino") one day race. Milan is also the traditional finish for the final stage of the [Giro d'Italia](/wiki/Giro_d%27Italia "Giro d'Italia"), which, along with the [Tour de France](/wiki/Tour_de_France "Tour de France") and the [Vuelta a España](/wiki/Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "Vuelta a España"), is one of cycling's three [Grand Tours](/wiki/Grand_Tour_(cycling) "Grand Tour (cycling)").

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Milano%2C_ca%27_granda%2C_01.jpg/250px-Milano%2C_ca%27_granda%2C_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milano,_ca%27_granda,_01.jpg)

Built in 1456 by [Francesco I Sforza](/wiki/Francesco_I_Sforza "Francesco I Sforza"), [Duke of Milan](/wiki/Duchy_of_Milan "Duchy of Milan"), the *[Ca' Granda](/wiki/University_of_Milan#Headquarters_–_City_Centre_Campus "University of Milan")* designed by the [Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance") period architect [Filarete](/wiki/Filarete "Filarete") is the headquarters of the [University of Milan](/wiki/University_of_Milan "University of Milan").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/POLIMI_Piazza_Leonardo_da_Vinci_32_-_on_the_right_the_building_3.jpg/250px-POLIMI_Piazza_Leonardo_da_Vinci_32_-_on_the_right_the_building_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:POLIMI_Piazza_Leonardo_da_Vinci_32_-_on_the_right_the_building_3.jpg)

The [Polytechnic University of Milan](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Milan "Polytechnic University of Milan") is the city's oldest university, founded in 1863. It is frequently ranked as the best university in Italy.[[230]](#cite_note-topuniversities-232)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Bicocca_edificio_U12.jpg/250px-Bicocca_edificio_U12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bicocca_edificio_U12.jpg)

[University of Milan Bicocca](/wiki/University_of_Milan_Bicocca "University of Milan Bicocca"), established in 1998, is the city's newest university.

Milan is a major global centre of higher education teaching and research and has the second-largest concentration of higher education institutes in Italy after Rome. Milan's higher education system includes 7 universities, 48 faculties and 142 departments, with 185,000 university students enrolled in 2011 (approximately 11 percent of the national total)[[27]](#cite_note-University_and_research_in_Milan-29) and the largest number of university graduates and postgraduate students (34,000 and more than 5,000, respectively) in Italy.[[231]](#cite_note-233)

### Universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Universities")]

The [Polytechnic University of Milan](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Milan "Polytechnic University of Milan") is the city's oldest university, founded in 1863. With over 40,000 students, it is the largest [technical](/wiki/Technology_(disambiguation) "Technology (disambiguation)") university in Italy.[[232]](#cite_note-234) According to the [QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings") for the subject area 'Engineering & Technology', it ranked as the 13th best in the world in 2022.[[233]](#cite_note-QS_World_University_Rankings-235) It ranked 6th worldwide for Design, 9th for Civil and Structural Engineering, 9th for Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, and 7th for Architecture.[[233]](#cite_note-QS_World_University_Rankings-235) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit) it is the best university in Italy and Southern Europe according to the QS Top Universities list, having consistently retained the first rank for many years.[[230]](#cite_note-topuniversities-232)

The [University of Milan](/wiki/University_of_Milan "University of Milan") (also known as the "State University"), founded in 1924,[[234]](#cite_note-236) is the largest public teaching and research university in the city.[[235]](#cite_note-237) The University of Milan is the sixth-largest university in Italy, with approximately 60,000 enrolled students and a teaching staff of 2,500.[[236]](#cite_note-238) The university ranks high in the fields of medicine, physics, mathematics, law, political science, sociology, philosophy, history, economics, statistics, geoscience and astronomy. Notable alumni such as former Italian Prime Minister [Silvio Berlusconi](/wiki/Silvio_Berlusconi "Silvio Berlusconi") and [Nobel](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") laureates have earned their degrees at the University of Milan. The Faculty of Medicine and Surgery at the University of Milan consistently ranks among the world's top 100 medical schools.[[237]](#cite_note-USNWR-239)[[238]](#cite_note-QS-Medicine-240) According to the global rankings of the [USNWR](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report_Best_Colleges_Ranking "U.S. News & World Report Best Colleges Ranking") in 2025, the University of Milan ranked 15th in the world in [Gastroenterology](/wiki/Gastroenterology "Gastroenterology") and [Hepatology](/wiki/Hepatology "Hepatology"),[[237]](#cite_note-USNWR-239) 43rd in [Oncology](/wiki/Oncology "Oncology"),[[237]](#cite_note-USNWR-239) 53rd in [Surgery](/wiki/Surgery "Surgery"), [Pharmacology](/wiki/Pharmacology "Pharmacology") and [Toxicology](/wiki/Toxicology "Toxicology"),[[237]](#cite_note-USNWR-239) 54th in [Clinical medicine](/wiki/Clinical_medicine "Clinical medicine"),[[237]](#cite_note-USNWR-239) 64th in [Radiology](/wiki/Radiology "Radiology"), [Nuclear medicine](/wiki/Nuclear_medicine "Nuclear medicine") and [Medical imaging](/wiki/Medical_imaging "Medical imaging"),[[237]](#cite_note-USNWR-239) 68th in [Cardiology](/wiki/Cardiology "Cardiology") and [Cardiovascular systems](/wiki/Circulatory_system "Circulatory system"),[[237]](#cite_note-USNWR-239) 77th in [Infectious diseases](/wiki/Infectious_diseases "Infectious diseases"),[[237]](#cite_note-USNWR-239) 78th in [Endocrinology](/wiki/Endocrinology "Endocrinology") and [Metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism"),[[237]](#cite_note-USNWR-239) 80th in [Neuroscience](/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience") and [Behavioural sciences](/wiki/Behavioural_sciences "Behavioural sciences"),[[237]](#cite_note-USNWR-239) and 98th in [Immunology](/wiki/Immunology "Immunology").[[237]](#cite_note-USNWR-239) In 2025 the University of Milan ranked 1st in Italy and 80th in the world in the field of [Medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine") according to the [QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings").[[238]](#cite_note-QS-Medicine-240)

The [University of Milano-Bicocca](/wiki/University_of_Milano-Bicocca "University of Milano-Bicocca"), established in 1998, is the city's newest institution of higher education in science and technology. Built over a once industrial area, today it enrolls more than 30,000 students, of whom more than 60% are female.[[239]](#cite_note-241) As its older parent institute, it is one of the most sought-after locations for medical students.[[240]](#cite_note-242) It ranked 82nd among over 300 young colleges in the 2020 *[Times Higher Education](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education "Times Higher Education")* World University Rankings.[[241]](#cite_note-243)

[Catholic University of the Sacred Heart](/wiki/Catholic_University_of_the_Sacred_Heart "Catholic University of the Sacred Heart") is the largest private teaching university in Europe[[242]](#cite_note-244) and the largest [Catholic University](/wiki/Catholic_University "Catholic University") in the world with 42,000 enrolled students.[[243]](#cite_note-245)[[244]](#cite_note-246) [Agostino Gemelli University Polyclinic](/wiki/Agostino_Gemelli_University_Polyclinic "Agostino Gemelli University Polyclinic") serves as the [teaching hospital](/wiki/Teaching_hospital "Teaching hospital") for the medical school of the Università Cattolica del Sacro Cuore and owes its name to the university founder, the [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan") [friar](/wiki/Friar "Friar"), physician and psychologist [Agostino Gemelli](/wiki/Agostino_Gemelli "Agostino Gemelli").[[245]](#cite_note-247)

[Bocconi University](/wiki/Bocconi_University "Bocconi University") is a private management and finance university established in 1902, ranking as the best university in Italy in its fields, and as one of the best in the world. In 2020, QS World University Rankings ranked the university seventh worldwide and third in Europe in business and management studies,[[246]](#cite_note-248) as well as first in economics and [econometrics](/wiki/Econometrics "Econometrics") outside the US and the UK.[[247]](#cite_note-249) *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")* ranked it the sixth-best business school in Europe in 2018.[[248]](#cite_note-250) Bocconi University also ranks as the fifth-best one-year MBA course in the world, according to the *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* 2017 ranking.[[249]](#cite_note-251)

[Vita-Salute San Raffaele University](/wiki/Vita-Salute_San_Raffaele_University "Vita-Salute San Raffaele University") is a private teaching medical university linked to the [San Raffaele Hospital](/wiki/San_Raffaele_Hospital "San Raffaele Hospital").[[250]](#cite_note-252)

[University Institute of Languages and Communication](/wiki/IULM_University "IULM University") (also known as "University IULM") is a private teaching university established in 1968, later renamed from its original name "University Institute of Languages of Milan", becoming first Italian university offering courses on [public relations](/wiki/Public_relation "Public relation"); later it became a point of reference also for [business communication](/wiki/Business_communication "Business communication"); media and advertising; translation and interpreting; communication in culture and arts markets, tourism and fashion.[[251]](#cite_note-253)

### Art academies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Art academies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Il_Conservatorio_di_Musica_di_Milano.jpg/250px-Il_Conservatorio_di_Musica_di_Milano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Il_Conservatorio_di_Musica_di_Milano.jpg)

[Milan Conservatory](/wiki/Milan_Conservatory "Milan Conservatory")

Milan is also well known for its fine arts and music schools. The Milan Academy of Fine Arts (Brera Academy) is a public [academic institution](/wiki/Academic_institution "Academic institution") founded in 1776 by Empress Maria Theresa of Austria; the [New Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/Nuova_Accademia_di_Belle_Arti_Milano "Nuova Accademia di Belle Arti Milano") is the largest private art and design university in Italy;[[252]](#cite_note-254) the [European Institute of Design](/wiki/Istituto_Europeo_di_Design "Istituto Europeo di Design") is a private university specialised in fashion, industrial and interior design, audio/visual design including photography, advertising and marketing and business communication; the [Marangoni Institute](/wiki/Istituto_Marangoni "Istituto Marangoni"), is a fashion institute with campuses in Milan, London and Paris; the [Domus Academy](/wiki/Domus_Academy "Domus Academy") is a private postgraduate institution of design, fashion, architecture, interior design and management; the Pontifical Ambrosian Institute of Sacred Music, a college of music founded in 1931 by the blessed cardinal A.I. Schuster, archbishop of Milan, and raised according to the rules by the Holy See in 1940, is—similarly to the Pontifical Institute of Sacred Music in Rome, which is consociated with—an Institute "ad instar facultatis" and is authorised to confer university qualifications with canonical validity[[253]](#cite_note-255) and the Milan Conservatory, a college of music established in 1807, currently Italy's largest with more than 1,700 students and 240 music teachers.[[254]](#cite_note-256)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main article: [Transport in Milan](/wiki/Transport_in_Milan "Transport in Milan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Shared_electric_cars_at_Piazza_Duca_d%27Aosta%2C_Milan.jpg/250px-Shared_electric_cars_at_Piazza_Duca_d%27Aosta%2C_Milan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shared_electric_cars_at_Piazza_Duca_d%27Aosta,_Milan.jpg)

[Carsharing](/wiki/Carsharing "Carsharing") cars in [Piazza Duca d'Aosta](/wiki/Piazza_Duca_d%27Aosta "Piazza Duca d'Aosta")

Milan is one of the key transport nodes of Italy and southern Europe. Its [central railway station](/wiki/Milano_Centrale_railway_station "Milano Centrale railway station") is Italy's second, after [Rome Termini railway station](/wiki/Rome_Termini_railway_station "Rome Termini railway station"), and Europe's eighth busiest.[[255]](#cite_note-grandistazioni-257)[[256]](#cite_note-258) The [Malpensa](/wiki/Milan_Malpensa_Airport "Milan Malpensa Airport"), [Linate](/wiki/Linate_Airport "Linate Airport") and [Orio al Serio](/wiki/Orio_al_Serio_International_Airport "Orio al Serio International Airport") airports serve the Greater Milan, the largest metropolitan area in Italy.
