[Azienda Trasporti Milanesi](/wiki/Azienda_Trasporti_Milanesi "Azienda Trasporti Milanesi") (ATM) is the Milanese municipal transport company; it operates five [metro](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") lines, 18 tram lines, 131 bus lines, four [trolleybus](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus") lines and one [people mover](/wiki/People_mover "People mover") line, carrying about 776 million passengers in 2018.[[257]](#cite_note-259) Overall the network covers nearly 1,500 km (932 mi) reaching 46 municipalities.[[258]](#cite_note-260) Besides public transport, ATM manages the interchange [parking lots](/wiki/Parking_lot "Parking lot") and other transport services, including [bike sharing](/wiki/BikeMi "BikeMi") and [carsharing](/wiki/Carsharing "Carsharing") systems.[[259]](#cite_note-261)

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Rail")]

#### Underground

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Underground")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/M4_San_Babila_appena_inaugurata.jpg/250px-M4_San_Babila_appena_inaugurata.jpg)](/wiki/File:M4_San_Babila_appena_inaugurata.jpg)

[Milan Metro](/wiki/Milan_Metro "Milan Metro") is the largest rapid transit system in Italy in terms of length, number of stations and ridership; and the fifth longest in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and the eighth in the [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe").[[260]](#cite_note-262)

The [Milan Metro](/wiki/Milan_Metro "Milan Metro") is the [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system serving the city and surrounding municipalities. The network consists of five lines ([M1](/wiki/Milan_Metro_Line_1 "Milan Metro Line 1"), [M2](/wiki/Milan_Metro_Line_2 "Milan Metro Line 2"), [M3](/wiki/Milan_Metro_Line_3 "Milan Metro Line 3"), [M4](/wiki/Milan_Metro_Line_4 "Milan Metro Line 4") and [M5](/wiki/Milan_Metro_Line_5 "Milan Metro Line 5")), with a total network length of 112 kilometres (70 mi), and a total of [125 stations](/wiki/List_of_Milan_Metro_stations "List of Milan Metro stations"), mostly underground.[[261]](#cite_note-Milan-figs-263) It has a daily ridership of 1.15 million,[[262]](#cite_note-rep3sep-264) the largest in Italy as well as one of the largest in Europe.

The architectural project of the Milan Metro, created by [Franco Albini](/wiki/Franco_Albini "Franco Albini") and [Franca Helg](/wiki/Franca_Helg "Franca Helg"), and the signs, designed by [Bob Noorda](/wiki/Bob_Noorda "Bob Noorda"), received the [Compasso d'Oro](/wiki/Compasso_d%27Oro "Compasso d'Oro") award in 1964.[[263]](#cite_note-265) In the European Union, it is the seventh-largest network in terms of kilometres.[[264]](#cite_note-266)

#### Suburban

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Suburban")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Milano_staz_Porta_Venezia_TSR_linea_S6.JPG/250px-Milano_staz_Porta_Venezia_TSR_linea_S6.JPG)](/wiki/File:Milano_staz_Porta_Venezia_TSR_linea_S6.JPG)

A [TSR](/wiki/Treno_Servizio_Regionale "Treno Servizio Regionale") train at [Milano Porta Venezia railway station](/wiki/Milano_Porta_Venezia_railway_station "Milano Porta Venezia railway station") on the [Milan Passerby Railway](/wiki/Milan_Passerby_Railway "Milan Passerby Railway")

As of September 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit), the [Milan suburban railway service](/wiki/Milan_suburban_railway_service "Milan suburban railway service"), operated by [Trenord](/wiki/Trenord "Trenord"), comprises 12 [S lines](/wiki/S-train "S-train") connecting the metropolitan area with the city centre, with possible transfers to all the metro lines. Most S lines run through the [Milan Passerby Railway](/wiki/Milan_Passerby_Railway "Milan Passerby Railway"), commonly referred to as "il Passante" and mostly served by double-decker trains every 4/8 minutes in the central underground section, between Porta Vittoria and Lancetti.[[265]](#cite_note-267)

#### National and international trains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: National and international trains")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Milan_CentralStation_016_4294.jpg/250px-Milan_CentralStation_016_4294.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milan_CentralStation_016_4294.jpg)

[Milano Centrale railway station](/wiki/Milano_Centrale_railway_station "Milano Centrale railway station")

Milan Central station, with 110 million passengers per year, is the largest and [eighth-busiest railway station in Europe](/wiki/List_of_busiest_railway_stations_in_Europe "List of busiest railway stations in Europe") and the second busiest in Italy after [Roma Termini](/wiki/Roma_Termini "Roma Termini").[[255]](#cite_note-grandistazioni-257) Milano Centrale railway station is the largest railway station in Europe by volume.[[266]](#cite_note-268) [Milano Cadorna](/wiki/Milano_Cadorna_railway_station "Milano Cadorna railway station") and [Milano Porta Garibaldi](/wiki/Milano_Porta_Garibaldi_railway_station "Milano Porta Garibaldi railway station") stations are, respectively, the seventh- and the eleventh-busiest stations in Italy, mostly served by regional and suburban trains.[[255]](#cite_note-grandistazioni-257)

Since the end of 2009, two [high-speed train](/wiki/High-speed_rail "High-speed rail") lines link Milan to Rome, [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples") and [Turin](/wiki/Turin%E2%80%93Milan_high-speed_railway "Turin–Milan high-speed railway"), considerably shortening travel times with other major cities in Italy. Since 2016, a further high-speed line connects the city to Brescia,[[267]](#cite_note-269) as part of the line towards Verona and Venice, which is still under construction,[[268]](#cite_note-270) while parts of the line towards Genoa are underway, as of May 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit).[[269]](#cite_note-271) Milan is served by direct international trains to Nice, Marseille, Lyon, Paris, Lugano, Geneva, Bern, Basel, Zurich and Frankfurt, and by overnight sleeper services to Munich and Vienna (ÖBB).[[270]](#cite_note-272) Since 2011, [Trenord](/wiki/Trenord "Trenord") has operated both Trenitalia and LeNord regional trains in Lombardy, carrying over 750,000 passengers on more than 50 routes every day.[[271]](#cite_note-273)[[272]](#cite_note-274)

[Trenitalia](/wiki/Trenitalia "Trenitalia") and [S.N.C.F.](/wiki/S.N.C.F. "S.N.C.F.") provide high speed services services to [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), stopping at [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon") and [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin").[[273]](#cite_note-275)[[274]](#cite_note-276)

### Buses and trams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Buses and trams")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Milano_tram_piazza_Cavour.jpg/250px-Milano_tram_piazza_Cavour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milano_tram_piazza_Cavour.jpg)

Intersecting trams under the arcs of [Porta Nuova medieval gate](/wiki/Porta_Nuova,_Gate_of_Milan "Porta Nuova, Gate of Milan"). This type of historical tram is also used in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), United States[[275]](#cite_note-277)

The [city tram network](/wiki/Trams_in_Milan "Trams in Milan") consists of approximately 160 kilometres (99 mi) of track and 18 lines, and is Europe's most advanced light rail system.[[276]](#cite_note-278) Bus lines cover over 1,070 km (665 mi). Milan also has [taxi](/wiki/Taxicab "Taxicab") services operated by private companies and licensed by the City Council of Milan. The city is also a key node for the national road network, being served by all the major highways of Northern Italy. Numerous long-distance bus lines link Milan with many other cities and towns in Lombardy and throughout Italy.[[277]](#cite_note-279)

### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Airports")]

Main article: [Airports of Milan](/wiki/Airports_of_Milan "Airports of Milan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Milan_malpensa_terminal_airport.jpg/250px-Milan_malpensa_terminal_airport.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milan_malpensa_terminal_airport.jpg)

[Milan Malpensa Airport](/wiki/Milan_Malpensa_Airport "Milan Malpensa Airport")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Bergamo_airport_terminal.jpg/250px-Bergamo_airport_terminal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bergamo_airport_terminal.jpg)

Departures area of the [Milan Bergamo Airport](/wiki/Milan_Bergamo_Airport "Milan Bergamo Airport")

In the surroundings of Milan, there are three airports dedicated to normal civilian traffic (Milan Malpensa Airport and Milan Linate Airport, managed by SEA, and Milan Bergamo Airport by SACBO).

Overall, the Milan airport system handles traffic of over 51.4 million passengers and around 700,000 tons of goods every year and is the first in Italy in terms of passenger volume and cargo volume (the second Italian airport system is Rome with 44.4 million passengers in 2023).[[278]](#cite_note-280) The Milan Malpensa airport, with over 700 thousand tons, confirms the national leadership, processing 70% of the country's air cargo.[[279]](#cite_note-281)

* The intercontinental [hub](/wiki/Spoke%E2%80%93hub_distribution_paradigm "Spoke–hub distribution paradigm") of [Milan Malpensa Airport](/wiki/Milan_Malpensa_Airport "Milan Malpensa Airport") (MXP) is Italy's second-busiest airport, after [Rome Fiumicino Airport](/wiki/Rome_Fiumicino_Airport "Rome Fiumicino Airport"), with 24.1 million passengers served in 2023 and Italy's busiest for freight and cargo, handling about 700,000 tons of international freight in 2022. Malpensa Airport is the largest [international airport](/wiki/International_airport "International airport") in northern Italy, serving [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy"), Piedmont and [Liguria](/wiki/Liguria "Liguria"), as well as the Swiss [canton of Ticino](/wiki/Canton_of_Ticino "Canton of Ticino"). The airport is located 49 kilometres (30 mi) north-west of Milan,[[280]](#cite_note-AIP-282) in the [province of Varese](/wiki/Province_of_Varese "Province of Varese") next to the [Ticino](/wiki/Ticino_(river) "Ticino (river)") river dividing Lombardy and Piedmont. Malpensa airport is ninth in the world and sixth in Europe for the number of countries served with direct scheduled flights[[281]](#cite_note-283) It is connected to Milan by the [Malpensa Express](/wiki/Malpensa_Express "Malpensa Express") railway service and by various bus lines.[[282]](#cite_note-284) The airport is located inside the [Parco naturale lombardo della Valle del Ticino](/wiki/Parco_naturale_lombardo_della_Valle_del_Ticino "Parco naturale lombardo della Valle del Ticino"), a [nature reserve](/wiki/Nature_reserve "Nature reserve") included by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") in the [World Network of Biosphere Reserves](/wiki/World_Network_of_Biosphere_Reserves "World Network of Biosphere Reserves").[[283]](#cite_note-285)[[284]](#cite_note-286)
* [Milan Linate Airport](/wiki/Milan_Linate_Airport "Milan Linate Airport") (LIN) is Milan's city airport, less than 8 kilometres (5 mi) from central Milan, and is mainly used for domestic and short-haul international flights. It served 8.6 million passengers in 2023 ranking as the eighth airport in Italy for passenger traffic.[[285]](#cite_note-287) Linate Airport is a focus city for [ITA Airways](/wiki/ITA_Airways "ITA Airways") (with Rome Fiumicino Airport as the main hub), and is connected the centre of Milan via the [M4 blue metro line](/wiki/Milan_Metro_Line_4 "Milan Metro Line 4").
* [Milan Bergamo Airport](/wiki/Milan_Bergamo_Airport "Milan Bergamo Airport") (BGY) is mainly used for low-cost, charter and cargo flights.[[286]](#cite_note-288) The airport is located in [Orio al Serio](/wiki/Orio_al_Serio "Orio al Serio"), 3.7 km (2.3 mi) south-east of [Bergamo](/wiki/Bergamo "Bergamo") and 45 km (28 mi) north-east of Milan. It is one of [Ryanair](/wiki/Ryanair "Ryanair")'s three main operating bases, along with [Dublin Airport](/wiki/Dublin_Airport "Dublin Airport") and [London Stansted Airport](/wiki/London_Stansted_Airport "London Stansted Airport").[[287]](#cite_note-289) It served 14.7 million passengers in 2023.[[288]](#cite_note-290) A bus service operated by ATB connects to the airport, about 10 minutes from the [Bergamo railway station](/wiki/Bergamo_railway_station "Bergamo railway station").[[289]](#cite_note-291)

Lastly, [Bresso Airfield](/wiki/Bresso_Airfield "Bresso Airfield") is a [general aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") airport, operated by Aero Club Milano.[[290]](#cite_note-292) Since 1960, the airport mostly serves as a general aviation airfield for flying club activity, tourist flights and air taxi.[[291]](#cite_note-293) It also hosts a base of the state helicopter emergency service *Elisoccorso*.[[292]](#cite_note-294)

### Cycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Cycling")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Bike_share.jpg/250px-Bike_share.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bike_share.jpg)

[BikeMi](/wiki/BikeMi "BikeMi") station and bikes along Corso Garibaldi

The bicycle is becoming an increasingly important mode of transportation in Milan. Since 2008, the implementation of a city-wide network of bike paths has been initiated, to fight congestion and air pollution. During the COVID pandemic in 2019, 35 km of bike lanes were realized on short notice, to relieve pressure on the subway occupation.[[293]](#cite_note-295)

The [bike sharing](/wiki/Bike_sharing "Bike sharing") system BikeMi operates throughout most of the city and has become increasingly popular. In addition, several commercial dockless bicycle and scooter sharing services are widely available.

## International relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: International relations")]

### Twin towns – sister cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Twin towns – sister cities")]

See also: [List of twin towns and sister cities in Italy](/wiki/List_of_twin_towns_and_sister_cities_in_Italy "List of twin towns and sister cities in Italy")

Milan is [twinned](/wiki/Sister_city "Sister city") with:[[294]](#cite_note-gemellaggi-296)

* [![Brazil](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/05/Flag_of_Brazil.svg/40px-Flag_of_Brazil.svg.png)](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), Brazil, since 1961
* [![United States](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a4/Flag_of_the_United_States.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_United_States.svg.png)](/wiki/United_States "United States") [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), United States, since 1962
* [![France](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/c/c3/Flag_of_France.svg/40px-Flag_of_France.svg.png)](/wiki/France "France") [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon"), France, since 1967
* [![Russia](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/f3/Flag_of_Russia.svg/40px-Flag_of_Russia.svg.png)](/wiki/Russia "Russia") [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg"), Russia, since 1967
* [![Germany](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/ba/Flag_of_Germany.svg/40px-Flag_of_Germany.svg.png)](/wiki/Germany "Germany") [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), Germany, since 1969
* [![United Kingdom](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom_%281-2%29.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom_%281-2%29.svg.png)](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), United Kingdom, since 1974
* [![Senegal](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Flag_of_Senegal.svg/40px-Flag_of_Senegal.svg.png)](/wiki/Senegal "Senegal") [Dakar](/wiki/Dakar "Dakar"), Senegal, since 1974
* [![China](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Flag_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China.svg.png)](/wiki/China "China") Shanghai, China, since 1979
* [![Japan](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/9e/Flag_of_Japan.svg/40px-Flag_of_Japan.svg.png)](/wiki/Japan "Japan") [Osaka](/wiki/Osaka "Osaka"), Japan, since 1981
* [![Israel](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Flag_of_Israel.svg/40px-Flag_of_Israel.svg.png)](/wiki/Israel "Israel") [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"), Israel, since 1997
* [![Palestine](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Flag_of_Palestine.svg/40px-Flag_of_Palestine.svg.png)](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine") [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem"), Palestine, since 2000
* [![Canada](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Flag_of_Canada_%28Pantone%29.svg/40px-Flag_of_Canada_%28Pantone%29.svg.png)](/wiki/Canada "Canada") [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), Canada, since 2003
* [![Poland](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/1/12/Flag_of_Poland.svg/40px-Flag_of_Poland.svg.png)](/wiki/Poland "Poland") [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), Poland, since 2003
* [![Australia](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Flag_of_Australia_%28converted%29.svg/40px-Flag_of_Australia_%28converted%29.svg.png)](/wiki/Australia "Australia") [Melbourne](/wiki/City_of_Melbourne "City of Melbourne"), Australia, since 2004
* [![South Korea](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Flag_of_South_Korea.svg/40px-Flag_of_South_Korea.svg.png)](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") [Daegu](/wiki/Daegu "Daegu"), South Korea, since 2015

The partnership with Saint Petersburg was suspended in 2012 (a decision taken by the city of Milan), because of the prohibition of the Russian government on "homosexual propaganda".[[295]](#cite_note-297) However, it was later restored and as of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milan&action=edit), St. Petersburg is still listed on Milan's official list of twin towns.[[294]](#cite_note-gemellaggi-296)
